proposals.title: Sensory Precision as a Causal Driver of Attentional Deployment: An ERP Investigation of Crossmodal Spatial Attention
proposals.motivation: Traditional models of attention posit that top-down or stimulus-driven attentional deployment modulates sensory processing, enhancing perceptual precision. However, the reverse causal direction—where inherent or induced changes in sensory precision themselves drive where attention is allocated—remains largely unexplied. Investigating this possibility is crucial for a complete, mechanistic understanding of attention, challenging the unidirectional framework dominant in pre-2026 cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that artificially manipulating the baseline sensory precision of a spatial location (e.g., via stochastic resonance or adaptation) will causally bias the subsequent deployment of spatial attention towards that location, even in the absence of explicit cueing or salient stimuli.
proposals.method: We will employ a crossmodal spatial attention paradigm combining vision and touch, using high-density EEG to measure event-related potentials (ERPs). In a series of experiments, we will first induce location-specific changes in tactile precision (e.g., via subthreshold noise or adapting stimuli) on one hand. Participants will then perform a visual discrimination task where targets appear with equal probability at locations corresponding to the precision-enhanced vs. control hand. Crucially, no informative cue will precede the target. We will analyze ERP markers of attentional preparation (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) and early sensory processing (P1, N1) to determine if the precision manipulation biases preparatory attention and subsequent visual processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct electrophysiological evidence for sensory precision as a causal antecedent of attentional deployment. It will challenge and refine models of attentional control by establishing a bidirectional link between perception and attention. Furthermore, it will extend Martin Eimer's established research on the spatial specificity and control mechanisms of crossmodal attention into a novel causal domain.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by Eimer and others has detailed how attention affects sensory precision, systematically testing the inverted causal direction—precision driving attention—using direct neural measures in a crossmodal context is a novel and non-obvious approach. It moves beyond correlation to test a fundamental assumption about the directionality of perception-attention interactions.

proposals.title: Attentional Selection as a Consequence of Flat, Non-Hierarchical Probabilistic Inference: An ERP Investigation
proposals.motivation: Dominant theories of attention, particularly in vision, often implicitly or explicitly rely on hierarchical processing architectures, where attention modulates information flow between lower and higher cortical areas. However, emerging theoretical frameworks in computational neuroscience, such as predictive processing and active inference, propose that perception is fundamentally a process of probabilistic inference. This project aims to test a radical, non-hierarchical instantiation of this idea, asking whether core attentional phenomena can be explained without invoking a processing hierarchy, thereby challenging a foundational assumption in cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key electrophysiological signatures of spatial attention (e.g., the N2pc component for visual selection) will be elicited in a task where target detection is driven solely by probabilistic inference within a flat, non-hierarchical generative model, demonstrating that hierarchical structure is not a necessary condition for attentional selection to emerge.
proposals.method: We will design a visual search task where stimuli are defined by complex, multi-feature conjunctions governed by known, experimenter-defined probabilities. Participants will learn these statistical regularities. Crucially, the task structure will be modeled computationally using a flat (single-layer) Bayesian network or a Markov Random Field, where predictions and prediction errors are computed laterally across the visual field, not hierarchically. We will record high-density EEG to measure established ERP components of spatial attention (N2pc, Pd, ADAN) as participants perform the search. The critical analysis will correlate the amplitude and latency of these components with trial-by-trial variables derived from the flat model's inference process, such as local surprise (prediction error) and posterior certainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a crucial empirical test of a fundamental architectural principle in theories of perception and attention. It will either demonstrate the sufficiency of non-hierarchical models for explaining attentional ERP effects, challenging mainstream views, or it will provide evidence that hierarchical processing is indispensable for the neural dynamics of attention, thereby strengthening existing frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While probabilistic models of attention exist, they are almost universally hierarchical. Testing a strictly flat model that still produces classic spatial attention ERP effects is a novel and theoretically provocative approach that directly bridges abstract computational principles and detailed neurophysiological measures.

proposals.title: Modulating perceptual precision: A free-energy principle model of non-spatial feature-based attention and its electrophysiological correlates
proposals.motivation: Martin Eimer's pre-2026 work has extensively characterized the ERP signatures of spatial attention, revealing its preparatory and modulatory mechanisms. However, attention can also be directed to non-spatial features like color or motion, a process less well-understood in terms of formal computational principles. The free-energy framework (Friston, 2010), which conceptualizes perception as minimizing prediction error, offers a powerful but underexplored formalism for modeling how feature-based attention optimizes sensory processing by modulating the precision (reliability) of predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that feature-based attention can be formalized within the free-energy framework as a top-down optimization of the precision weights assigned to sensory prediction errors associated with task-relevant features, and that this optimization will be reflected in specific, quantifiable modulations of early (e.g., N2pc) and later (e.g., SPCN) visual ERP components.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical generative model based on the free-energy principle, where higher levels encode prior beliefs about feature values (e.g., red vs. blue). Attention is implemented as increasing the precision (inverse variance) of these priors. Model simulations will generate trial-by-trial predictions for behavioral response times and accuracy. These will be tested against human EEG data from a novel visual search task where participants attend to a cued color or motion direction among distractors. We will use multivariate decoding of EEG data to track the trial-wise strength of the attended feature representation and correlate this with the model's estimated precision parameter.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational account of feature-based attention, bridging the gap between high-level theoretical frameworks and detailed electrophysiological measures. It will extend Eimer's research program on the mechanisms of attention beyond the spatial domain and offer a unified perspective on how attention, as precision optimization, shapes perception across different feature dimensions.
proposals.novelty: While the free-energy principle has been applied to spatial attention and oddball paradigms, its explicit application to model the dynamics of non-spatial feature-based attention, directly linked to specific ERP components like the N2pc, is a novel synthesis of computational neuroscience and cognitive electrophysiology.

proposals.title: Inverting the Spotlight: Reconstructing Internal Spatial Priors and Precision Weighting from ERP Signatures of Crossmodal Attention
proposals.motivation: Attention research has extensively mapped how internal goals and cues modulate the processing of external stimuli. However, the inverse problem—inferring the precise internal model (e.g., spatial prior distributions) and precision beliefs an observer uses to guide attention—remains unsolved. Solving this is crucial for a complete computational account of attentional control and for understanding individual differences in perceptual strategies, particularly in complex, multisensory environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatiotemporal dynamics of event-related potentials (ERPs) elicited during spatial cueing tasks, especially the lateralized components (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) and sensory N1 modulations, contain sufficient information to reconstruct an observer's internal spatial prior and their relative precision weighting for visual versus tactile modalities.
proposals.method: We will adapt a spatial cueing paradigm with probabilistic cues, manipulating cue validity and modality (vision, touch). Behavioral data (RT, accuracy) and high-density EEG will be recorded. We will formalize the 'inverse problem' using hierarchical Bayesian modeling. The model will treat the observer's internal spatial prior (e.g., a Gaussian distribution over cued locations) and modality-specific precision parameters as latent variables. We will fit this model jointly to behavioral responses and trial-by-trial ERP amplitudes (focusing on established lateralized components) using variational Bayesian inference or Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods, thereby inferring the latent parameters that best explain the observed neural and behavioral data patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal method for quantitatively inferring an individual's attentional tuning functions and crossmodal precision beliefs from neural data. It will bridge the gap between descriptive ERP effects of attention and generative computational models, offering a new tool for characterizing attentional states and their neural implementation with greater specificity.
proposals.novelty: While ERP components are well-established markers of attentional deployment, using their detailed properties to solve the inverse problem and reconstruct a quantitative internal model is a novel, model-driven approach that moves beyond correlational analysis to computational state estimation.

proposals.title: Electrophysiological Signatures of Attentional Control Deficits in Spatial Neglect: An ERP Study of Preparatory and Target-Evoked Components
proposals.motivation: Spatial neglect, a debilitating neuropsychological deficit, is characterized by a failure to attend to contralesional space. Pre-2026 research by Eimer and others has established that covert spatial attention in healthy individuals is indexed by specific ERP components, such as the early directing attention negativity (EDAN), anterior directing attention negativity (ADAN), and lateralized readiness potentials (LRPs) during preparation, and the N2pc and P3 during target processing. Applying this precise electrophysiological framework to neglect patients can objectively quantify the specific stages of attentional control (e.g., attentional orienting, engagement, disengagement) that are impaired, moving beyond behavioral measures alone.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with left spatial neglect will show a specific reduction or absence of contralesionally-directed preparatory ERP components (ADAN/LRP) and a delayed or attenuated N2pc to targets in the left hemifield, pinpointing deficits in the voluntary generation of attentional control signals and their subsequent implementation.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms (e.g., attending to quadrants) for use with right-hemisphere stroke patients with left neglect and matched controls. High-density EEG will be recorded. The primary analysis will compare the amplitude and latency of the ADAN, LRP (following central directional cues), and the N2pc/P3 (following lateralized visual targets) between cued left and right hemifields. Statistical parametric mapping (SPM) and robust cluster-based permutation tests will be used to identify significant spatiotemporal differences in these components between groups and conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a fine-grained, component-based model of the breakdown of spatial attention in neglect, linking specific ERP markers to distinct functional deficits. It will offer objective electrophysiological biomarkers for assessing the severity of neglect and potentially evaluating rehabilitation outcomes. The findings will bridge cognitive neuroscience and clinical neuropsychology by applying a well-validated experimental framework to a pathological population.
proposals.novelty: While ERPs have been used in neglect, this proposal systematically applies Eimer's specific framework of preparatory (ADAN) and target-evoked (N2pc) control components, derived from healthy attention studies, to dissect the disorder. The explicit focus on preparatory states as a potential core deficit moves beyond simply measuring processing of neglected stimuli.

proposals.title: The resilience of cross-modal attentional links: An ERP investigation under conditions of spatial and intermodal divided attention
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Eimer and others has robustly established cross-modal links in spatial attention, showing that directing attention to a location in one modality (e.g., vision) enhances processing in another (e.g., touch) at that same location. However, these findings primarily come from tasks where attention is focused on a single location or modality. In natural environments, attention is often divided. It remains unknown whether these cross-modal links are robust or break down under more demanding, divided attention conditions, a critical test for their functional role in real-world perception.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the electrophysiological signatures of cross-modal spatial attention (specifically, early sensory ERP components like the N1 and P1) will persist but be attenuated when attention is divided between two spatial locations or between two sensory modalities, compared to focused attention conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ high-density EEG to record event-related potentials (ERPs) in a spatial cueing paradigm. Participants will perform a speeded discrimination task on visual (Gabor patches) and tactile (vibrations) targets presented to the left or right. In separate blocks, we will manipulate attentional focus: 1) Focused attention (cue to one modality at one location), 2) Spatial divided attention (cue to one modality at both locations), and 3) Intermodal divided attention (cue to both modalities at one location). We will analyze early visual (occipital P1, N1) and somatosensory (N140) components for spatial attention effects, comparing amplitudes for cued versus uncued stimuli across the three attention conditions to quantify the cost of division.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct electrophysiological evidence quantifying how cross-modal attentional links are modulated by divided attention. It will clarify whether these links reflect a mandatory, capacity-limited supramodal control system or more flexible, independent resource pools. The results will bridge the gap between highly controlled laboratory demonstrations of cross-modal attention and its operation in complex, multi-source environments.
proposals.novelty: While previous ERP studies by Eimer have mapped cross-modal links under focused attention, systematically testing their resilience under divided attention—both spatially and between modalities—within a single design is a novel and critical next step. This moves beyond establishing the existence of links to probing the architecture and limits of the underlying control mechanisms.

proposals.title: Causal Necessity of the Posterior Parietal Cortex for Cross-Modal Spatial Attention: A TMS-ERP Investigation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 ERP research by Eimer and others has robustly established cross-modal links in spatial attention, showing that attending to a location in one modality (e.g., touch) modulates early sensory processing in another (e.g., vision). However, these correlational findings cannot determine which cortical areas are causally necessary for generating these cross-modal attentional effects. Identifying these neural substrates is critical for a complete mechanistic model of supramodal attention control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the right posterior parietal cortex (PPC) is causally necessary for the generation of cross-modal spatial attention effects, such that transient disruption via TMS will selectively abolish the attention-related modulations of early somatosensory and visual ERP components (e.g., the N140 and P1).
proposals.method: We will combine single-pulse TMS with high-density ERP recording. In a spatial cueing paradigm, participants will attend to left or right locations for tactile or visual targets. On critical trials, a TMS pulse will be delivered to the right PPC (or a control site) at a specific latency (e.g., 100ms post-cue) to disrupt attentional control processes. The primary dependent measures will be the amplitude of modality-specific, attention-sensitive ERP components (tactile N140, visual P1) at contralateral electrodes, compared between TMS and no-TMS conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct causal evidence for the cortical areas necessary for supramodal spatial attention, moving beyond correlation. It will clarify whether a common parietal locus controls attention across senses or if distinct, interconnected regions are involved. The findings will constrain theoretical models of the attention control network.
proposals.novelty: While TMS has been used to study unimodal attention, its application to directly test the causal basis of well-established cross-modal ERP effects is novel. The precise temporal combination of TMS perturbation with ERP measurement allows us to test the necessity of a candidate area at a specific processing stage, a method not yet applied to this specific question in Eimer's work.

proposals.title: Structural connectivity predicts functional efficacy: DTI-based white matter pathways linking visual and somatosensory cortices account for individual differences in crossmodal attentional ERP effects
proposals.motivation: Martin Eimer's pre-2026 ERP research has robustly established crossmodal links in spatial attention between vision and touch, showing that attending to a location in one modality facilitates processing in the other. However, the neuroanatomical substrate underlying these functional links and the substantial individual variability in their strength remains poorly understood. Mapping the specific white matter pathways that connect visual and somatosensory cortical regions is a critical next step to explain how structural architecture constrains and enables these well-documented attentional effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the microstructural integrity (indexed by fractional anisotropy, FA) of specific white matter tracts connecting early visual (e.g., V1/V2) and somatosensory (e.g., S1) cortices will be positively correlated with the magnitude of crossmodal attentional facilitation, as measured by ERP components (e.g., N1, P1) in spatial cueing tasks.
proposals.method: We will combine DTI with established ERP paradigms. Participants will complete a spatial cueing task where auditory cues direct attention to a location, followed by visual or tactile targets, while 64-channel EEG is recorded. In a separate session, high-resolution DTI (e.g., 60+ diffusion directions, b=1000 s/mm²) will be acquired on a 3T MRI scanner. Probabilistic tractography (using FSL's FDT toolbox, specifically 'probtrackx') will be used to map and quantify the connectivity strength and FA of pathways between functionally defined visual and somatosensory ROIs. Individual ERP effect sizes (cue-valid vs. invalid difference waves) will be entered into a multiple regression model with tract-specific FA and connectivity probability as predictors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence linking individual differences in the structural connectivity of specific crossmodal pathways to the efficacy of crossmodal spatial attention. It will move the field from describing functional links to explaining their biological basis and variability. The findings will establish a structural-functional model for crossmodal attention, with implications for understanding neuroplasticity and rehabilitation in sensory deficits.
proposals.novelty: While Eimer's work has meticulously characterized the functional ERP signatures of crossmodal attention, integrating these measures with individual DTI-based tractography to predict effect sizes is a novel, causally informative step. This approach moves beyond correlating brain activity with behavior to testing how the underlying wiring of the brain determines the efficiency of attentional control across senses.

proposals.title: A Computational Model of Asymmetric Crossmodal Gating: Simulating ERP Signatures of Visual-Tactile Spatial Attention
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 ERP studies, including seminal work by Eimer and colleagues, robustly demonstrate that spatial attention to one modality (e.g., vision) influences processing in another (e.g., touch), but these crossmodal links are often asymmetric. The neural mechanisms underlying this asymmetry remain poorly understood. Computational modeling offers a powerful tool to formalize and test competing theories of how attentional control signals from different modalities interact in a common spatial framework.
proposals.hypothesis: The asymmetric gating observed in crossmodal attention ERP components (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) can be simulated by a neural network model where top-down spatial attention signals from vision and touch have differential strengths and temporal dynamics in their influence on a shared supramodal spatial priority map.
proposals.method: We will develop a spiking neural network model using a pre-2026 architecture, such as a leaky integrate-and-fire network with divisive normalization, structured to include separate visual and tactile input streams, modality-specific spatial attention modules, and a convergent supramodal priority map. The model will be trained and tested using simulated trial sequences mirroring Eimer's spatial cueing paradigms. We will fit the model's output (spike rates, simulated local field potentials) to empirical ERP waveforms (e.g., the early directing attention negativity) by optimizing parameters governing the strength and latency of crossmodal connections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, mechanistic account of asymmetric crossmodal attention, moving beyond descriptive ERP effects. It will generate testable predictions for future experiments regarding the locus and timing of crossmodal interactions. The model will serve as a unifying framework for interpreting disparate findings on the spatial specificity of preparatory attention.
proposals.novelty: While computational models of visual attention exist, applying them to simulate specific, well-characterized ERP components from crossmodal studies is novel. The explicit goal of capturing the empirically observed asymmetry between vision and touch in a single, parsimonious model represents a significant advance over purely unimodal or symmetric modeling approaches.

proposals.title: ERP Signatures of Sustained Spatial Attention: A Test of the Serial Transient Shifts Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: A long-standing model in cognitive neuroscience posits distinct neural mechanisms for transient (phasic) and sustained (tonic) visual attention. However, recent theoretical challenges suggest sustained attention may not be a continuous state but rather a rapid series of transient attentional shifts. Resolving this debate is critical for understanding the fundamental architecture of attentional control. Martin Eimer's pre-2026 work on the spatial specificity of preparatory ERP components (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) provides an ideal foundation to test this hypothesis with high temporal precision.
proposals.hypothesis: If sustained attention is merely a series of transient shifts, then the electrophysiological correlates of sustained spatial attention (e.g., contralateral negativity during a delay period) will decompose into a sequence of discrete, time-locked components resembling those elicited by explicit transient cues. A distinct sustained mechanism would predict a qualitatively different, stable electrophysiological pattern.
proposals.method: We will employ high-density EEG to record ERPs in a modified spatial cueing paradigm. Participants will maintain attention at a cued location over extended delays (2-4 seconds) while performing a demanding discrimination task on infrequent targets. Crucially, we will use a temporal response function (TRF) analysis to model the continuous EEG signal as a linear combination of (a) responses to the initial transient cue and (b) a putative oscillatory or stochastic process representing serial re-triggering. We will compare this model's fit to data against models containing a separate, sustained neural source, using Bayesian model comparison. Analysis will focus on established lateralized components (ADAN, LDAP, N2pc, SPCN) and their temporal dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, model-based test of a foundational dichotomy in attention theory. It will clarify whether sustained spatial attention indices reflect a unique cognitive/neural process or an emergent property of a transient system. The findings will directly inform computational models of attention and have implications for understanding deficits in clinical populations characterized by sustained attention problems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has contrasted transient and sustained attention blocks, this study innovatively uses fine-grained temporal modeling of ERPs during sustained periods to test if their signature is mathematically reducible to a series of transient events. It moves beyond descriptive correlation to a formal test of mechanistic equivalence.

proposals.title: Invalid Cues as a Trigger for Broad Perceptual Monitoring: An ERP Investigation of Spatial Attention
proposals.motivation: The classic Posner cueing paradigm robustly shows performance costs for invalidly cued targets, interpreted as the cost of reorienting a focused attentional spotlight. However, this assumes a purely serial, shift-based model. Recent pre-2026 work on distributed attention and the flexibility of attentional control suggests that invalid cues might, under specific conditions, induce a qualitatively different, broader preparatory state, potentially beneficial for monitoring unpredictable events.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in tasks where target locations are highly unpredictable following an invalid cue, the invalidity will trigger a shift from a focused, spatially specific preparatory state to a broader monitoring state, leading to faster or more accurate detection of targets appearing at novel, uncued locations compared to a neutral baseline, as indexed by modulations of attentional ERP components (e.g., ADAN, LDAP).
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms, using EEG/ERP. In a visual task, central arrow cues will predict a target's likely quadrant (80% valid). Crucially, on invalid trials, targets will appear not at the single opposite location, but equiprobably at one of many locations within a large, uncued 'ring-shaped' region. This design contrasts invalid-cue trials (inducing broad monitoring) with valid-cue (focused attention) and neutral-cue (distributed baseline) trials. We will analyze cue-locked lateralized components (ADAN, LDAP) to track the spatial specificity of preparation, and target-locked P3 and N2pc to assess subsequent processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct electrophysiological evidence for the adaptive, strategic nature of attentional control, demonstrating that 'invalid' cues are not merely disruptive but can proactively trigger a beneficial, broad monitoring state. It will challenge the monolithic view of the cue validity effect and refine models of spatial attention by delineating the conditions under which invalidity leads to costs versus a strategic benefit.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has studied costs of invalidity, this proposal directly tests the novel, non-obvious prediction that invalidity can be beneficial by forcing a strategic switch in the *mode* of attention (focused vs. broad), a hypothesis not systematically investigated in pre-2026 ERP studies of spatial cueing.

proposals.title: The interactive control of spatial attention: Dissociating and integrating symbolic and spatial cue information in the visual ERP
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on attentional control, including seminal work by Eimer, has often used pure symbolic cues (e.g., arrows, letters) to study endogenous attention and pure peripheral cues to study exogenous attention. This separation creates an artificial dichotomy, as real-world cues often carry both symbolic meaning and inherent spatial information. The interaction between these two information streams in guiding spatial selection remains poorly understood, limiting models of attentional control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that symbolic and spatially predictive information in a compound cue interact non-additively to shape preparatory attentional states, with spatial predictability modulating early sensory-specific ERP components (e.g., the early directing attention negativity, EDAN) and symbolic meaning influencing later, more abstract control-related components (e.g., the late directing attention positivity, LDAP).
proposals.method: We will employ a spatial cueing paradigm with EEG/ERP recording. Cues will be centrally presented symbols (e.g., shapes or colored letters) that vary orthogonally in their symbolic spatial instruction (e.g., 'left' vs. 'right' meaning) and their probabilistic spatial predictiveness (e.g., 80% vs. 50% valid). Participants will detect visual targets in left or right hemifields. We will analyze cue-locked ERP components known from Eimer's prior work (EDAN, ADAN, LDAP) and target-locked N2pc and P1 components to track the time course and neural correlates of attentional deployment and selection efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more nuanced, integrated model of attentional control by mapping how different information types in a cue are processed and combined. It will clarify the functional significance of established ERP markers (EDAN, LDAP) by dissociating their sensitivity to symbolic vs. probabilistic information. The findings will bridge research on endogenous cueing and probabilistic learning.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have manipulated cue validity, they have not orthogonally combined a symbolic instruction with an independent level of spatial predictability within the same cue stimulus. This design uniquely allows us to test for interactive, rather than merely additive, effects on the neural mechanisms of spatial attention.

proposals.title: Source Localization of the Midline Negativity in Visual Spatial Attention: A Combined ERP and sLORETA Study
proposals.motivation: The midline negativity (Nd), a component of the event-related potential (ERP) linked to spatial attention, is a robust marker of attentional selection. However, its neural generators remain ambiguous, limiting our understanding of the cortical networks underlying spatial orienting. Identifying these sources is a critical step in linking ERP markers to specific brain systems involved in attentional control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the midline negativity elicited during valid spatial cueing is generated by a network including the supplementary motor area (SMA) and anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), reflecting their role in the top-down control of spatial attention.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigm with central symbolic cues and lateralized visual targets. High-density (64-channel) EEG will be recorded. The Nd component will be isolated by subtracting ERPs on invalid trials from valid trials in the cue-target interval. Source localization will be performed using standardized Low-Resolution Brain Electromagnetic Tomography (sLORETA), a validated, assumption-free algorithm for solving the inverse problem. Statistical analyses will compare source activity between conditions across the estimated cortical voxels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, non-invasive source localization of the visual spatial attention Nd, moving beyond scalp topography. It will clarify whether this component originates from medial frontal control areas or more posterior sensory regions, thereby constraining theoretical models of attentional selection.
proposals.novelty: While Eimer's work has meticulously characterized the functional properties of the Nd, applying source localization techniques like sLORETA to this specific component in a classic spatial attention task is novel. It bridges the gap between detailed ERP effect characterization and underlying cortical anatomy.

proposals.title: Dissociating Automatic and Controlled Processes in Visual Sequence Learning: A Dual-Task ERP Study of Attentional Resource Dependence
proposals.motivation: Event-related potential (ERP) studies have identified distinct neural correlates of visual sequence learning, such as the N2b and P3 components, but their functional interpretation remains debated. It is unclear whether these components reflect automatic, resource-independent processes or controlled, attention-demanding operations. Resolving this is critical for understanding the cognitive architecture of implicit learning and its interaction with attention, a core theme in cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the later P3 component associated with sequence violations is attention-dependent and will be attenuated under dual-task load, whereas the earlier N2b component, linked to deviant detection, will remain intact, indicating it is a more automatic, resource-independent process.
proposals.method: We will adapt a standard visual serial reaction time (SRT) task, where stimuli appear in a repeating spatial sequence. Participants will perform this primary task while concurrently engaging in a secondary auditory oddball task, known to draw on central executive resources. High-density EEG will be recorded. The key manipulation is the attentional load (single vs. dual-task). We will analyze ERP waveforms time-locked to sequence violations, focusing on mean amplitudes of the N2b (200-300 ms) and P3 (300-500 ms) components over parietal-occipital sites, using repeated-measures ANOVA with factors Task (single, dual) and Sequence (standard, deviant).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct functional dissociation between ERP markers of sequence learning based on their attentional resource dependence. It will clarify the role of controlled processing in implicit learning and bridge research on sequence learning with established literature on the resource theory of attention. The findings will constrain theoretical models of how learning unfolds across hierarchical processing stages.
proposals.novelty: While dual-task paradigms have been used with behavioral SRT tasks, systematically applying this logic to the fine-grained temporal resolution of ERPs in visual-spatial sequence learning is novel. It directly tests the resource-dependence of specific neural components, moving beyond correlational evidence to a more causal, functional account.

proposals.title: Reversing the Predictive Map: ERP Signatures of Attention When Invalid Cues are the Norm
proposals.motivation: Classical spatial cueing paradigms establish a strong predictive link between a cue and a subsequent target at the same location, making valid trials frequent. This design conflates the effects of spatial orienting with the effects of predictive probability and expectation. To isolate the neural mechanisms of voluntary spatial attention from those of probabilistic prediction, it is critical to dissociate the most frequent event from the cued location.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core event-related potential (ERP) markers of spatial attention (specifically, the early directing attention negativity, EDAN, and the anterior directing attention negativity, ADAN) will be significantly attenuated or will reverse polarity when the cued location is the less probable target location, demonstrating that these components reflect a combination of spatial selection and top-down expectation.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms (e.g., 'Attending to quadrants...') but invert the standard probability manipulation. In a visual spatial attention task, central symbolic cues will indicate the likely target location, but targets will appear at the opposite, uncued location on 80% of trials (invalid-frequent condition). We will compare ERP waveforms (focusing on posterior P1/N1 and fronto-central ADAN components) from this condition to a standard valid-frequent condition. EEG will be recorded from 64 channels, and analysis will focus on mean amplitude measures in predefined time windows relative to cue onset.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test whether established ERP indices of attentional orienting are fundamentally driven by spatial alignment or by the violation or confirmation of expectation. It will provide a clearer functional decomposition of the ADAN and related components, informing models of cognitive control in spatial attention. The findings will clarify the interplay between endogenous spatial selection and predictive coding in the brain.
proposals.novelty: While many studies manipulate cue validity, the novel inversion—making the invalid location the statistically frequent event—creates a powerful conflict between symbolic instruction and statistical learning that has not been systematically explored in ERP research on spatial attention.

proposals.title: ERP Evidence for the Interaction of Identity and Location Processing in Visual Attention: A Priming Study
proposals.motivation: Classical models of visual attention and object recognition often treat 'what' (identity) and 'where' (location) processing as functionally and neurally separable streams. However, recent behavioral and neuroimaging work suggests potential interactions. A direct test using the high temporal resolution of event-related potentials (ERPs) is needed to determine if these processes are truly independent or interact during early perceptual stages, challenging a fundamental tenet of visual neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: If identity and location processing are separable, priming effects for identity and location should be additive in a combined task. If they interact, priming for one dimension will modulate the priming effect for the other, leading to a non-additive interaction in behavioral and ERP measures.
proposals.method: We will design a sequential priming paradigm where participants respond to a target defined by both its identity (e.g., letter 'X' vs. 'O') and its location (e.g., left vs. right quadrant). In different blocks, the task will require a combined judgment, a location-only judgment, or an identity-only judgment. We will record EEG to analyze established ERP components linked to spatial attention (N2pc) and object processing (N170/N250). The critical analysis will be a 2x2 factorial design examining the interaction between identity priming and location priming on reaction times, accuracy, and component amplitudes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial electrophysiological evidence on the temporal dynamics of identity and location integration. It will clarify whether the 'what' and 'where' pathways operate independently or interact during attentional selection, thereby informing and potentially revising models of visual object recognition and spatial attention.
proposals.novelty: While previous ERP studies by Eimer and others have examined spatial and non-spatial attention separately, this proposal directly tests their interaction within a single, integrated task using a priming design, moving beyond the established literature on the spatial specificity of preparatory attention.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Spatial Attention: An ERP Study of the Time Course of Facilitation and Inhibition
proposals.motivation: While Martin Eimer's pre-2026 work has robustly established the spatial specificity of attentional preparation in vision and touch, the precise temporal dynamics of these preparatory states remain less clearly mapped. Understanding the time course of attentional facilitation at cued locations and the potential concurrent inhibition at uncued locations is crucial for refining models of attentional control, such as the Premotor Theory of Attention. This study aims to systematically trace the build-up and resolution of these attentional modulations over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that attentional facilitation at the cued location, indexed by enhanced early sensory ERP components (e.g., P1, N1), will show a specific time course, peaking at an optimal cue-target onset asynchrony (CTOA). Concurrently, inhibition at uncued locations, potentially reflected in later components or behavioral costs, will exhibit a distinct and possibly more sustained temporal profile.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatial cueing paradigm with visual stimuli, systematically varying CTOA across a wide range (e.g., 100, 200, 400, 800, 1200 ms). Central arrow cues will indicate the likely target location with high validity. Participants will detect or discriminate targets appearing at cued or uncued locations. High-density EEG will be recorded to analyze CTOA-dependent modulations of lateralized ERP components linked to attentional selection (e.g., ADAN, LDAP, and early sensory components). Behavioral performance (RT, accuracy) will be analyzed as a function of CTOA and cue validity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a high-resolution, millisecond-level map of the temporal dynamics of spatial attentional control, directly linking preparatory brain states to their behavioral consequences. It will clarify whether facilitation and inhibition operate on identical or dissociable time scales, offering critical evidence for theories of attentional prioritization. The findings will extend Eimer's established work on spatial specificity by adding a definitive temporal dimension.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have used a few discrete CTOAs, this proposal systematically samples a continuous range to model the precise time course, allowing us to distinguish the dynamics of facilitation from inhibition, a distinction often inferred but not directly mapped in prior ERP studies of spatial attention.

proposals.title: Mapping the Cortical Network of Spatial Priming and Inhibition: An fMRI Investigation of Attentional Control
proposals.motivation: Martin Eimer's ERP research has robustly characterized the temporal dynamics of spatial attention, revealing distinct preparatory states and crossmodal links. However, the precise cortical network orchestrating these effects—particularly the interplay between frontal control regions and sensory-specific areas during spatial priming and inhibition of return (IOR)—remains spatially underspecified. Identifying this network is crucial for a complete model of attentional control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial priming and IOR are implemented by a distributed network including the frontal eye fields (FEF), intraparietal sulcus (IPS), and sensory-specific visual and somatosensory cortices, with the FEF and IPS showing sustained, preparatory activity that modulates sensory processing based on spatial cue validity.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms (e.g., attending to quadrants) for a 3T fMRI block and event-related design. Participants will respond to visual or tactile targets at cued or uncued locations. Whole-brain BOLD data will be analyzed using SPM12. We will employ general linear models (GLM) to contrast activity during validly vs. invalidly cued trials and during preparatory intervals. Functional connectivity (psychophysiological interaction, PPI) analyses will test for increased coupling between FEF/IPS and sensory cortices during effective spatial priming.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution spatial map of the brain network underlying the attentional effects extensively documented by Eimer's ERP work. It will bridge the temporal precision of ERPs with the spatial localization of fMRI, offering a more complete systems-level understanding of how preparatory attention and IOR are implemented in the human brain.
proposals.novelty: While Eimer's work precisely charts the *when* of spatial attention using ERPs, this proposal directly tests the *where* by applying fMRI to his well-validated paradigms, moving from characterizing effects to localizing their neural sources and interactions within the attentional network.

proposals.title: Backward Priming of Spatial Attention: ERP Evidence for Retrospective Attentional Facilitation
proposals.motivation: Research on preparatory spatial attention, including Eimer's work, has established that cues prospectively modulate the processing of subsequent targets. However, the temporal dynamics of attention are not fully understood. It remains unknown whether a target stimulus, once identified, can act as a retrospective cue to prime the processing of a subsequent, spatially congruent probe, revealing a potential backward-priming mechanism in attentional selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a spatially attended target will act as an effective cue, facilitating the processing of a subsequent probe appearing at the same location, and that this backward-priming effect will be reflected in modulations of early sensory ERP components (e.g., N1, P1).
proposals.method: We will adapt a standard spatial cueing paradigm, reversing the sequence. On each trial, a target (e.g., a shape discrimination task) will appear at a cued or uncued location. Following target offset and a variable stimulus-onset asynchrony (SOA: 100-500 ms), a task-irrelevant visual probe (e.g., a Gabor patch) will appear at either the same location as the target or a different location. High-density EEG will be recorded to measure ERP components time-locked to the probe onset. The key manipulation is the spatial congruency between the target and the probe location.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct electrophysiological test of backward priming in spatial attention, extending models of attentional control beyond purely prospective preparation. It will clarify whether attentional facilitation can be triggered retrospectively by a prior target event, offering new insights into the temporal flexibility of attentional resources.
proposals.novelty: While forward cueing is well-studied, systematically using the target as a cue for a subsequent probe to examine retrospective attentional effects is a novel reversal of the standard paradigm. This directly tests the symmetry and temporal boundaries of attentional facilitation.

proposals.title: Inhibitory Control in Spatial Attention: Dissociating Facilitation and Suppression via Unexpected Location Targets
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 ERP research on spatial attention, including Eimer's work, has robustly demonstrated attentional facilitation at cued locations. However, the relative contribution of active suppression of non-cued, unexpected locations remains less clear. This proposal directly tests whether the classic N2pc and Pd components, typically linked to target selection and distractor suppression, are driven by inhibitory processes when the task requires attending to an unexpected location.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when a target systematically appears at an unexpected, non-cued location, the resulting attentional effects will be primarily driven by inhibitory control mechanisms, reflected in enhanced Pd components and a reduced or absent N2pc, compared to a standard valid-cue condition.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's spatial cueing paradigms, using lateralized visual stimuli. In the critical 'Inverted Expectation' block, a central cue (e.g., an arrow) will predict a likely target location with high probability (e.g., 80%), but the target will actually appear at the opposite, unexpected location on all trials. This will be compared to standard valid (cue-target same location) and invalid (cue-target different location) blocks. EEG will be recorded from 64 channels, and ERP analysis will focus on the N2pc (contralateral negativity 200-300ms post-stimulus) and Pd (contralateral positivity) components at posterior sites, using established subtraction methods (ipsilateral minus contralateral activity).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, within-experiment dissociation of facilitatory and inhibitory processes in spatial orienting. It will clarify whether attentional selection in dynamic environments can be predominantly driven by the suppression of expected-but-irrelevant locations, advancing models of attentional control from a primarily facilitatory framework to one integrating proactive inhibition.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined invalid trials, this design inverts the standard logic by making the 'invalid' location the constant target, isolating the preparatory and reactive processes of suppressing a highly expected but never-relevant location, a novel manipulation in the ERP literature.

proposals.title: The Topography of Visual-Spatial Attention: High-Density ERP Mapping of Attentional Gradients and Discrete Foci
proposals.motivation: Classical models of visual-spatial attention, often based on cuing paradigms and ERP components like the N2pc, treat the attentional 'focus' as a unitary, discrete entity. However, pre-2026 work by Eimer and others on attending to rings and quadrants suggests attentional resources can be distributed across non-contiguous regions. Resolving whether the focus is a single point, a gradient field, or a configurable distribution is critical for constraining neural and computational models of attentional selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial attention operates not as a single, discrete focus but as a dynamically configurable field, which can manifest as a smooth gradient, multiple discrete peaks, or a uniform distribution depending on task demands and spatial configuration.
proposals.method: We will employ high-density (128-channel) EEG to record ERPs while participants perform a visual discrimination task. Spatial sampling will be maximized using a circular array of 16+ potential target locations at equal eccentricity. Attentional deployment will be cued to single locations, pairs of locations (varying in angular separation), and full ring-shaped regions. Analysis will focus on the topography and amplitude of early sensory (P1/N1) and selection-related (N2pc) components across the electrode array. We will use surface Laplacian transforms and statistical parametric mapping (SPM) to model the scalp distribution of attentional modulation as a function of cue type.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution spatiotemporal map of attentional modulation across a full visual hemifield, directly testing discrete vs. field-like models. It will specify the conditions under which attention forms a unitary focus versus a distributed pattern, thereby linking ERP research to theoretical models of population coding in visual cortex.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have compared attention to points versus large regions, the systematic, high-density sampling of multiple intermediate spatial configurations to model the attentional 'field' is novel. This moves beyond the binary question of 'focus vs. diffuse' to characterize the precise spatial geometry of attentional deployment.

proposals.title: Disentangling Cortical and Strategic Origins of the Visual Meridian Effect: An ERP Study of Spatial Attention Deployment
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 ERP research, including Eimer's work, robustly shows that shifting attention across a visual meridian (vertical/horizontal midline) incurs a cost, often attributed to the architecture of retinotopic cortical maps. However, it remains unclear if this cost is a mandatory consequence of cortical organization or a task-specific strategic bias, such as the need to reconfigure spatial coordinates or attentional sets when targets appear in different hemifields or quadrants. Resolving this is critical for understanding the fundamental constraints on visual spatial selection.
proposals.hypothesis: The meridian effect is not a fixed architectural constraint but is modulated by task-specific strategic factors. Specifically, the cost of crossing a meridian will be reduced or eliminated when the task encourages a unitary, global attentional set rather than a selection based on discrete spatial regions.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms with lateralized visual targets, measuring ERP components (N2pc, P1, LDAP) as indices of attentional deployment. The key manipulation will be the spatial structure of the attended region. In separate blocks, participants will attend to discrete locations/quadrants (inducing a local set) versus ring-shaped or large rectangular regions that span meridians (inducing a global set). Reaction times and ERP latencies/amplitudes for within-region versus cross-meridian shifts will be compared across these conditions to isolate strategic from architectural contributions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence distinguishing hard-wired cortical from flexible strategic accounts of the visual meridian effect. It will refine models of spatial attention by specifying the conditions under which cortical boundaries limit performance. The findings will also inform the design of visual search displays and human-computer interfaces by clarifying the costs of meridian crossings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented the meridian effect, no pre-2026 study has systematically manipulated the *attentional set* (local vs. global) to test its causal role in generating the cost. This moves beyond correlation to experimentally dissociate the potential sources of the effect.

proposals.title: Attentional Momentum in Visual Space: ERP Evidence for Sequential Dependencies in Covert Orienting
proposals.motivation: Research on spatial attention has predominantly examined single, isolated shifts. However, in natural behavior, attention moves in sequences. Pre-2026 work on sequential effects in reaction times suggests performance is influenced by previous trial history, but the underlying neural mechanisms and their spatiotemporal dynamics remain poorly understood. This study aims to bridge this gap by investigating how the direction of a prior attentional shift modulates the neural preparatory state and efficiency of a subsequent shift.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that repeating the direction of an attentional shift across trials creates 'attentional momentum,' leading to faster behavioral responses and enhanced modulations of attention-related ERP components (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) compared to trials where the shift direction reverses.
proposals.method: We will employ a modified spatial cueing paradigm where participants covertly orient attention to lateralized visual targets over extended sequences. The key manipulation is the relationship between shift directions (leftward vs. rightward) across consecutive trials. We will record high-density EEG to analyze trial-by-trial fluctuations in established ERP markers of spatial attention (e.g., early directing attention negativity (EDAN), anterior directing attention negativity (ADAN), late directing attention positivity (LDAP)) and lateralized readiness potentials (LRPs). Statistical analyses will focus on comparing neural preparatory activity and target-evoked responses between direction-repeat and direction-switch trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct electrophysiological characterization of attentional momentum, revealing how the brain's attentional control systems are dynamically shaped by recent history. It will extend models of attentional control from single shifts to sequential operations, offering a more ecologically valid account. The findings will also inform theoretical debates on whether sequential effects arise from facilitation of repeated operations or inhibition of recently disengaged locations.
proposals.novelty: While sequential effects in behavior are known, this study uniquely tracks their neural implementation using well-established, spatially specific ERP components from Eimer's prior work, moving beyond behavioral measures to reveal the dynamic preparation and consequences of attentional shifts over time.

proposals.title: Mapping the Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Attentional Tuning: A Reverse-Correlation Analysis of Sequential History Effects
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including seminal work by Eimer, has established that spatial attention acts as a dynamic filter, but its precise tuning properties as shaped by recent stimulus history remain poorly characterized. Traditional ERP studies using discrete cues and targets reveal attentional effects but lack the granularity to reconstruct the continuous, trial-by-trial evolution of the attentional receptive field (aRF). This gap limits our understanding of how attention adapts to statistical regularities in the environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sequential history of target locations systematically warps the spatial profile of the aRF, sharpening it around the recent mean location and biasing it along the recent trajectory, and that these tuning properties can be directly visualized using psychophysical reverse correlation.
proposals.method: We will adapt a spatial cueing paradigm where on each trial, a target (e.g., a Gabor patch) appears at one of many possible locations within a circular array. Crucially, target location will be determined by a random walk, creating structured sequential dependencies. We will employ a reverse correlation technique: by treating the binary sequence of target-present/absent behavioral responses (or response times) as the output and the recent history of spatial input as the input, we will compute classification images. This involves convolving the binary response vector with the spatial stimulus history matrix to estimate the linear kernel that best predicts attentional deployment, effectively mapping the aRF's weighting function across space and time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, model-free visualization of the attentional receptive field as it is shaped by temporal context, moving beyond mean amplitude ERP effects. It will quantify the spatial extent, asymmetry, and inertia of attentional tuning, offering a novel link between behavioral adaptation phenomena and the neural mechanisms of spatial selection studied in Eimer's prior ERP work.
proposals.novelty: While reverse correlation is established in low-level vision, its application to map the attentional filter defined by sequential history in a spatial attention task is novel. This approach moves from measuring the *consequences* of attention (ERP effects) to characterizing the dynamic *filter* itself, a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Spatial Attention Modulates the Breakthrough of Unconscious Visual Stimuli: An ERP Study Combining Continuous Flash Suppression and Sequential Cuing
proposals.motivation: A core debate in pre-2026 cognitive neuroscience concerns the extent to which attention can operate on stimuli that do not reach conscious awareness. While Martin Eimer's prior work has established robust ERP markers of preparatory spatial attention, and psychophysical methods like Continuous Flash Suppression (CFS) can render stimuli invisible, their combination is rare. This gap limits our understanding of whether the spatial attentional mechanisms identified in conscious tasks are similarly engaged for suppressed stimuli.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that covert spatial attention, directed by a valid pre-cue, will facilitate the processing and reduce the suppression time of a subsequent visual target rendered invisible via CFS, and that this facilitation will be reflected in modulations of early visual ERP components (e.g., N1, N2pc) even before target awareness.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's sequential spatial cuing paradigm for use with CFS. On each trial, a central auditory or tactile cue will validly or invalidly indicate the likely quadrant of a subsequent target (a Gabor patch). The target will be presented to one eye while dynamic Mondrian masks are presented to the other, inducing CFS. Participants will press a key upon target detection (breakthrough). We will measure suppression time (time to breakthrough) and record high-density EEG. The key analysis will compare ERP waveforms time-locked to target onset between validly and invalidly cued trials, focusing on components linked to spatial selection (N2pc) and early visual processing, prior to the behavioral response.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct electrophysiological evidence for or against the role of spatial attention in pre-conscious visual processing. It will bridge the literatures on the neural correlates of spatial attention and visual consciousness. The findings will clarify whether attentional selection mechanisms are contingent on conscious access or can operate independently within the visual hierarchy.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a well-established ERP spatial attention paradigm with a powerful psychophysical suppression technique (CFS) allows us to track the time-course of attentional modulation on stimuli held outside of awareness, moving beyond purely behavioral measures.

proposals.title: Decoding the Spatial Focus of Crossmodal Attention from Single-Trial EEG: A Machine Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Decades of ERP research, including seminal work by Eimer and others, have established how spatial attention modulates sensory processing. However, this mapping is largely one-way: we know the neural consequences of a known attentional focus. The inverse problem—reliably inferring the current focus of attention from the EEG signal alone—remains a significant challenge. Solving it would enable new, objective measures of covert attentional state with applications in cognitive neuroscience and brain-computer interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial distribution of attention to distinct visual field locations (e.g., quadrants or rings) generates unique, decodable patterns in the multivariate EEG signal, even in the absence of overt stimuli, and that these patterns generalize across sensory modalities when attention is crossmodally oriented.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms (e.g., attending to quadrants) to collect dense-array EEG data. Time-locked EEG epochs from the sustained attention period will be used as features. We will employ a linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and a non-linear support vector machine (SVM) with radial basis function kernels to classify attended location from single trials. A recurrent neural network (RNN) will be explored for decoding the temporal dynamics of attentional focus. Critical comparisons will be made between within-modality (visual) and crossmodal (vision-touch) attention decoding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, multivariate method for tracking covert spatial attention in real time, moving beyond traditional univariate ERP analyses. It will directly test the decodability of crossmodal attentional states, offering new insights into their shared neural representations. The project will establish a proof-of-concept for an EEG-based 'attention decoder'.
proposals.novelty: While ERP components like the N2pc index attention, decoding the specific attended location from pre-stimulus or sustained-attention EEG is non-trivial. Applying modern machine learning classifiers to this classic spatial attention problem, particularly to decode the locus of crossmodal attention, represents a significant methodological advance beyond the established ERP literature.

proposals.title: ERP Signatures of Preparatory Auditory Spatial Attention: A Sequential Location-Cueing Paradigm
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Eimer and others has established robust event-related potential (ERP) markers of preparatory visual spatial attention, such as the Early Directing Attention Negativity (EDAN) and Anterior Directing Attention Negativity (ADAN). However, the electrophysiological mechanisms of purely auditory spatial attention, particularly the preparatory control processes involved in selecting a sequence of locations, remain less understood. Extending the sequential location method to audition is crucial for developing a comprehensive, cross-modal theory of spatial attention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that directing auditory attention sequentially to different locations in space will elicit a sequence of lateralized ERP components (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) that reflect the dynamic updating of attentional focus, and that these components will show a different scalp topography and time course compared to their visual counterparts due to the distinct neural architecture of auditory spatial processing.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's visual sequential location-cueing paradigm to the auditory domain. Participants will fixate centrally while auditory spatial cues (e.g., brief tones from one of eight speakers arranged in a circle) indicate a sequence of two successive auditory target locations. High-density EEG will be recorded. The analysis will focus on cue-locked ERPs, specifically lateralized components (contralateral minus ipsilateral to the cued location) during the preparatory intervals. We will employ established spatial filtering and source localization techniques (e.g., sLORETA) available pre-2026 to characterize the neural generators of these components.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed ERP characterization of preparatory control states during sequential auditory spatial orienting. It will clarify whether supramodal or modality-specific control mechanisms govern spatial attention across sensory domains. The findings will directly inform models of auditory scene analysis and spatial hearing in complex environments.
proposals.novelty: While crossmodal links between vision and touch have been studied, applying the precise sequential location method—a gold standard in visual attention research—to a purely auditory spatial task is novel. This allows for a direct comparison of the temporal dynamics and neural substrates of attentional control between vision and audition.

proposals.title: Direct comparison of preparatory attentional states: ERP signatures of sequential location cues versus traditional spatial precues
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Eimer and others has established that both peripheral and central symbolic precues induce spatially specific preparatory states, measurable via lateralized event-related potentials (ERPs) like the ADAN/LDAP. A novel sequential location cueing method, where attention is guided through a series of locations, has also been shown to produce robust attentional effects. However, the functional equivalence and underlying control mechanisms of these different cueing types remain untested in a direct, within-subject design, limiting theoretical understanding of attentional control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequential location cues will elicit a distinct pattern of preparatory ERP components compared to traditional single-location precues, reflecting a more dynamic or sustained engagement of spatial attention control systems, even when the final cued location is identical.
proposals.method: We will employ a within-subjects design where participants perform a visual discrimination task. In separate blocks, a target's location will be cued either by a classic central arrow (symbolic precue), a peripheral highlight (peripheral precue), or a sequence of four dots sequentially illuminating a path ending at the target location (sequential cue). High-density EEG will be recorded. The primary analysis will compare the amplitude, latency, and scalp topography of established lateralized preparatory ERPs (ADAN, LDAP) and target-evoked N2pc components between cue types. Behavioral performance (RT, accuracy) will be analyzed to link neural preparatory states to functional outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural comparison of sequential and traditional spatial cueing, clarifying whether they engage common or dissociable control mechanisms. It will advance theories of attentional selection by testing if the *process* of orienting, not just the final attentional focus, shapes the preparatory neural state. The findings will have implications for models of dynamic real-world attention, where cues are often sequential (e.g., following a pointing gesture).
proposals.novelty: While both cueing methods have been studied in isolation, their direct comparison within a single experiment is novel. The critical test is whether an identical final attentional focus, achieved via different orienting processes, yields identical or dissociable preparatory neural signatures.

proposals.title: The Control of Supramodal Spatial Attention: ERP Signatures of Preparatory States Triggered by Unpredictable Crossmodal Cues
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Eimer and others has established robust crossmodal links in spatial attention, showing that cues in one modality (e.g., vision) can enhance processing of subsequent targets in the same location but a different modality (e.g., touch). However, these paradigms typically use predictable cue-target modality pairings. The critical, unresolved question is whether the attentional control system can generate a supramodal preparatory state when the target modality is unpredictable, requiring a truly abstract spatial representation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially informative cues will elicit modality-independent, location-specific preparatory brain activity (indexed by lateralized ERPs like the ADAN) even when the target modality is unpredictable, demonstrating a flexible, supramodal attentional control mechanism.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigm. On each trial, a central auditory or tactile cue (e.g., a tone or vibration to the left/right index finger) will indicate a left or right target location. Crucially, the target modality (visual or tactile) will be unpredictable on a trial-by-trial basis. We will record high-density EEG to analyze lateralized event-related potentials (ERPs) during the cue-target interval (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) and target-evoked components (e.g., N2pc, tactile N140). Behavioral performance (RT, accuracy) will be measured for all cue-target-modality combinations.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test the flexibility and abstraction of the attentional control system. It will determine if preparatory spatial attention can be fully decoupled from sensory-specific expectations, providing critical evidence for a genuinely supramodal attentional control architecture. The findings will bridge gaps between research on crossmodal links and theories of supramodal attention control.
proposals.novelty: While previous work examined crossmodal attention with predictable targets, this study introduces target modality uncertainty, moving beyond demonstrating links to probing the nature of the control signal itself. This unpredictability is key to isolating a modality-independent preparatory state.

proposals.title: Mapping the Supramodal and Modality-Specific Cortical Networks of Crossmodal Spatial Attention with High-Resolution fMRI
proposals.motivation: While ERP studies by Eimer and others have established the existence and temporal dynamics of crossmodal spatial attention links, the precise cortical networks underlying these effects remain poorly mapped. fMRI's superior spatial resolution is critical for identifying whether shared 'supramodal' fronto-parietal regions control attention across vision and touch, or if modality-specific areas are primarily involved, resolving a key theoretical debate in pre-2026 attention research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that directing attention to a common spatial location across vision and touch will activate a shared, supramodal fronto-parietal network, while also engaging distinct, modality-specific sensory regions in a preparatory state prior to target onset.
proposals.method: We will adapt established spatial cueing paradigms from Eimer's ERP work for a 3T fMRI scanner. Participants will receive tactile or visual cues indicating a left or right target location, followed by a target in the cued or uncued modality (vision/touch). A fast, event-related design will separate cue-related preparatory activity from target-evoked responses. Whole-brain BOLD data will be analyzed using General Linear Models (GLM) in SPM12 or FSL. Contrasts will identify networks for spatial orienting (cued vs. uncued location), modality-specific preparation (tactile-cue vs. visual-cue), and supramodal convergence (overlap of tactile and visual attention maps).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution spatial map of the preparatory brain states in crossmodal attention, directly linking the well-established ERP effects to their neural generators. It will critically inform models of attentional control by delineating the architecture of supramodal versus modality-specific attentional systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying fMRI's spatial precision to the well-controlled, temporally-defined preparatory phases of crossmodal attention tasks pioneered in ERP research, moving beyond correlational resting-state studies to reveal the causal architecture of active attentional networks.

proposals.title: Causal contributions of the posterior parietal cortex to crossmodal spatial attention: A combined TMS-ERP study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 ERP research by Eimer and others has established robust crossmodal links in spatial attention, showing that attending to a location in one modality (e.g., touch) facilitates processing of stimuli in another (e.g., vision) at that same location. However, these studies are correlational. The specific cortical regions that play a causal, controlling role in generating and maintaining these supramodal attentional templates remain unclear. Establishing causality is a critical next step for understanding the neural architecture of integrated spatial perception.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the right posterior parietal cortex (PPC) is causally necessary for the establishment of supramodal spatial attentional sets. Disrupting right PPC function with TMS during the preparatory period of a crossmodal attention task will selectively eliminate the characteristic ERP markers of spatial attention (e.g., the anterior directing attention negativity, ADAN) and impair behavioral performance for both visual and tactile targets at the attended location.
proposals.method: We will combine offline, repetitive TMS (e.g., 1 Hz stimulation for 10 minutes) with high-density ERP recording. In a within-subjects design, participants will perform a crossmodal spatial attention task after receiving real or sham TMS over the right PPC (targeting regions identified in prior fMRI studies of crossmodal attention). The task requires attending to a left or right location for tactile targets while ignoring visual distractors, or vice versa. We will analyze the effects of TMS on established ERP components indexing attentional preparation (ADAN, LDAP) and target processing (N2pc, tactile N140), as well as on reaction time and accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct causal evidence for the role of a specific cortical region (right PPC) in controlling supramodal spatial attention. It will bridge the gap between correlational ERP markers and their underlying neural generators, moving the field from describing effects to explaining their origin. The findings will constrain models of the fronto-parietal attention network by specifying which nodes are indispensable for crossmodal integration.
proposals.novelty: While TMS has been used to study unimodal attention, its application to directly test the causal foundations of crossmodal attentional links—as measured with the precise temporal resolution of ERPs—is novel. The proposal uniquely integrates Eimer's established ERP paradigms with a causal perturbation technique to answer a longstanding question in his research program.

proposals.title: The Lifespan Development of Crossmodal Spatial Attention: An ERP Investigation from Childhood to Old Age
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Eimer and others has established robust crossmodal links in spatial attention between vision and touch in adults, indexed by event-related potentials (ERPs) like the N2pc and ADAN. However, the developmental trajectory of these mechanisms is unknown. Understanding when and how these supramodal attentional systems emerge, stabilize, and potentially decline is crucial for a complete theory of attention and for informing interventions in developmental disorders and age-related cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural signatures of crossmodal spatial attention (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) will show a non-linear developmental trajectory, emerging in late childhood, reaching peak efficiency in young adulthood, and exhibiting specific declines in older age, with tactile cues showing a different developmental profile compared to visual cues.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms for vision and touch for use across ages. Participants from four age groups (children 8-12, adolescents 13-17, young adults 18-30, older adults 65+) will perform tasks where lateralized cues in one modality (e.g., tactile vibration) predict targets in the same or opposite modality (e.g., visual).
