High-density EEG will be recorded. Analysis will focus on established, pre-2026 ERP components: the ADAN and LDAP as indices of preparatory spatial attention, and the N2pc and tactile N140 for target selection. Effects will be analyzed for spatial specificity and crossmodal congruency across groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive ERP-based map of crossmodal attention development across the lifespan. It will reveal critical periods for the maturation of supramodal attentional control and identify which specific components are most vulnerable to aging. This will bridge developmental cognitive neuroscience and adult attention research, offering foundational data for theories of attentional development and plasticity.
proposals.novelty: While Eimer's work has meticulously detailed the architecture of crossmodal attention in adults, applying this precise ERP methodology systematically across the lifespan to trace its developmental origins and senescence is a novel and logical next step that has not been undertaken.

proposals.title: Behavioral and Neural Costs of Forced Crossmodal Attentional Decoupling
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Eimer and others has robustly established that spatial attention tends to couple across sensory modalities, facilitating processing of co-located multisensory events. However, the mechanisms and costs associated with *overriding* this default coupling are poorly understood. This proposal directly investigates the inverse scenario: the behavioral and neural consequences when attention is forced to decouple, a critical test of the flexibility and control of the crossmodal attention system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that forcing attention to decouple to distinct spatial locations in vision and touch will incur significant behavioral costs (slower RTs, reduced accuracy) and elicit distinct neural signatures, including modulations of early sensory ERP components (e.g., P1, N1) and later frontal-parietal activity linked to cognitive control.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms. On each trial, two sequential cues will instruct participants to attend to one location in vision (e.g., left visual field) and a *different* location in touch (e.g., right hand). Following this, visual and tactile stimuli will be presented simultaneously at the cued locations and their opposites. The primary measure is the performance (RT, d') for detecting infrequent targets in each modality under this 'decoupled' condition, compared to a baseline 'coupled' condition. High-density EEG will be recorded to analyze cue-related preparatory activity (e.g., LDAP, ADAN) and stimulus-evoked ERPs, focusing on modulations indicative of conflict and control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic quantification of the cost of crossmodal attentional decoupling, moving beyond the documentation of coupling effects. It will delineate the temporal dynamics and neural control mechanisms required to overcome the default integrated attentional state, offering a more complete model of multisensory spatial attention.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has studied how attention couples, this study explicitly manipulates the system to force it apart, testing its limits and control architecture. This 'inverse' approach is a novel and powerful way to understand the obligatory nature of crossmodal links.

proposals.title: Individual Differences in Automatic Response Activation Predict the Trainability of Early Visual ERP Components for Complex Shapes
proposals.motivation: Perceptual learning studies show training can modify early visual processing, but with high individual variability. Separately, cognitive neuroscience links individual differences in automatic response control (e.g., flanker effects) to frontal-striatal circuits. It remains unknown whether these stable cognitive traits predict the plasticity of early sensory filters, a link that could unify theories of automaticity and perceptual expertise.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals with stronger baseline automatic response activation (larger behavioral flanker/Simon effects) will exhibit greater training-induced modulations in early visual event-related potential (ERP) components (specifically the P1 and N1) during complex shape discrimination, reflecting a greater capacity for perceptual learning in early sensory cortices.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal design with EEG. First, individual differences in automatic activation will be quantified using a visual flanker task, measuring the behavioral congruency effect and its associated lateralized readiness potential (LRP). Participants will then undergo multi-session training on a demanding shape discrimination task using novel, parametrically morphed radial frequency patterns. Training-induced plasticity will be tracked via changes in the amplitude and latency of the P1 and N1 components over occipital sites in response to trained shapes. Analyses will correlate baseline flanker effect magnitude with the degree of ERP modulation post-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel predictive link between a core cognitive control trait and sensory cortical plasticity, moving beyond descriptive accounts of individual differences. It will challenge the view of early visual processing as a rigid, feed-forward stage by showing its malleability is systematically related to higher-order automatic processes.
proposals.novelty: While Eimer's prior work established spatial attention's effects on early ERPs, this proposal uniquely tests whether a *non-spatial*, domain-general automaticity trait predicts the *plasticity* of these same components for complex feature processing, fusing individual differences and perceptual learning literatures.

proposals.title: Extensive Perceptual Training Induces Early Sensory ERP Modulations for Complex Shape Discrimination
proposals.motivation: A long-standing debate in attention research concerns whether attention modulates early sensory processing or later postperceptual stages. ERP studies, including Eimer's work on spatial attention, show early sensory effects (e.g., N1) for simple features like location, but these effects are often absent for complex object properties, suggesting a postperceptual locus. This proposal tests whether this dichotomy is fixed or malleable, addressing a core theoretical question about the plasticity of attentional selection mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: With extensive, focused perceptual training, attention to complex, non-spatial shape features will elicit early sensory-evoked potential modulations (specifically, an enhanced N1 component), demonstrating that early sensory gating is not exclusive to simple features but can be extended through practice.
proposals.method: We will employ a visual discrimination task where participants attend to one of two complex, overlapping shapes (e.g., Greebles) at a fixed central location. High-density EEG will be recorded. The key manipulation is an extensive multi-session training regimen (10+ sessions) using adaptive psychophysical staircases to shape discrimination ability. We will analyze training-induced changes in ERP components (C1, P1, N1, N2pc, P3) time-locked to stimulus onset, focusing on the emergence of early sensory (N1) attention effects. Spatial cueing protocols will ensure attention is directed feature-based, controlling for location-based effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct electrophysiological evidence that the boundary between 'sensory' and 'postperceptual' attention is plastic. It will challenge static models of attentional selection by showing training can shift the neural locus of selection earlier in the processing stream, bridging literature on perceptual learning and attention.
proposals.novelty: While perceptual training is known to improve performance, demonstrating it can fundamentally alter the stage of attentional selection—moving complex feature attention into the realm of early sensory ERP modulations—is a novel and non-obvious prediction from the standard dichotomy.

proposals.title: Object-Grouping versus Spatial Window: Dissociating the Mechanisms of Quadrant-Based Visual Selection
proposals.motivation: The dominant 'zoom-lens' model of spatial attention posits that selection operates via a unitary, gradient-based spatial window. However, this model struggles to explain the efficiency of selecting complex, non-contiguous regions like quadrants. Drawing on pre-2026 work in Gestalt psychology and object-based attention, this proposal tests whether quadrant selection is better explained by object-grouping heuristics, a fundamental but untested alternative with implications for models of attentional control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that quadrant selection is primarily driven by object-based perceptual grouping (e.g., via common region or connectedness) rather than by the deployment of a purely spatial attentional window. When pitted against each other, Gestalt grouping cues will dominate spatial-probabilistic cues in guiding attentional deployment and modulating early sensory processing.
proposals.method: We will adapt established spatial cueing paradigms used by Eimer, employing event-related potentials (ERPs) to measure attentional effects (e.g., N2pc, Pd, early sensory modulations). In critical experiments, visual displays will contain elements that can be grouped into a quadrant based on Gestalt principles (e.g., common color, connected lines, common region defined by a subtle background texture), while spatial probability cues (e.g., symbolic central cues) will indicate a different, conflicting quadrant. The relative strength of ERP markers of attentional selection for the Gestalt-grouped quadrant versus the cued quadrant will be compared.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct electrophysiological evidence for or against an object-based mechanism in quadrant selection, challenging the canonical zoom-lens model. It will bridge the literature on spatial attention and perceptual organization, offering a more integrated framework for how the visual system selects complex spatial regions.
proposals.novelty: While object-based attention is well-studied for discrete objects, applying this framework to explain the selection of standard spatial regions (quadrants) is novel. It directly tests a long-held but unverified assumption that spatial cueing paradigms isolate a purely spatial mechanism.

proposals.title: Causal Disentanglement of Spatial and Feature-Based Selection in Ring Attention: A Synthetic Benchmark and ERP Validation
proposals.motivation: A central debate in visual attention research concerns whether selection is driven by spatial location, non-spatial features, or an interaction of both. Traditional ERP studies, including those on ring-shaped regions, struggle to fully dissociate these mechanisms due to inherent correlations in natural stimuli. A causal test requires a controlled environment where spatial and feature-based signals are independently manipulated, a gap this proposal addresses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ring attention operates via a hybrid selection mechanism, where initial spatial gating to the ring is followed by feature-based competition among items within the attended region. Purely spatial or purely feature-based models will fail to match human behavioral and neural data under controlled adversarial conditions.
proposals.method: We will generate synthetic visual scenes containing target and distractor objects defined by curvature, with their locations and features independently randomized. We will train and ablate computational models of ring attention (e.g., spatial transformer networks with feature gating) on this benchmark. Concurrently, we will conduct an ERP experiment where human participants perform a target detection task on identical stimuli, measuring components like N2pc (feature-based selection) and early directing attention negativity (EDAN; spatial selection). Model predictions will be directly compared to ERP effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, causal account of the selection mechanisms in ring attention, moving beyond correlational evidence. It will establish a novel synthetic benchmark for testing attention models and yield converging evidence from computational modeling and human electrophysiology, strengthening the theoretical framework for spatial and feature-based attention.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the adversarial synthesis of stimuli to *causally* disentangle selection pathways, combined with the direct integration of ablated neural network models and human ERP measures to test the same controlled conditions, a method not employed in prior attention research.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Neural Signature of Spatial Attention: An ERP Filter-Bank Approach to Complex Visual Configurations
proposals.motivation: Research on the spatial spotlight of attention has traditionally focused on simple, contiguous regions. However, real-world attention often targets complex, non-contiguous shapes. Pre-2026 ERP studies by Eimer and others on annuli and quadrants show distinct attentional modulations (e.g., N2pc, Pd), but it remains unknown whether these signatures for complex shapes reflect the integrated processing of the whole configuration or the linear sum of attention to its constituent spatial primitives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ERP signature of attending to a complex spatial configuration (e.g., an annulus) is not a simple linear sum of the signatures elicited by attending to its decomposed simpler elements (e.g., inner and outer bounding contours), but rather reflects a non-linear, integrative neural process specific to the configured shape.
proposals.method: We will adapt the signal-processing concept of a filter bank. In a series of ERP experiments, participants will perform spatial cueing tasks where targets appear within predefined visual regions. The 'complex configuration' condition will involve attending to an annulus. The 'filter bank' conditions will involve attending to its constituent elements: the inner circle, the outer circle, and the curved sector between them, presented in isolation or in controlled combinations. By comparing the topography, latency, and amplitude of attention-related ERP components (N2pc, Pd, CDA) across these conditions, we can model the complex shape's signature as a function of its parts. Statistical analyses will include linear mixed models and tests of additivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for decomposing the neural correlates of complex spatial attention, moving beyond a holistic description. It will advance our theoretical understanding of whether the visual system constructs complex attentional templates through linear summation or specialized configural processing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly importing the analytical logic of acoustic filter banks to visual spatial attention research, allowing for a principled decomposition of ERP signatures that has not been attempted in pre-2026 work on spatial selection.

proposals.title: Decoding the Spatial Geometry of Attentional Templates: An MVPA-fMRI Study of Quadrant vs. Ring Selection
proposals.motivation: Prior ERP work by Eimer and others has established that attention can be allocated to complex spatial configurations like quadrants or rings, eliciting distinct preparatory neural signatures. However, these univariate signals are ambiguous; they may reflect the attentional template itself or adaptation to repeated stimulus locations. A critical, unresolved question is whether the brain maintains a pure, stimulus-independent representation of the attended spatial region's geometry. This proposal addresses this gap by isolating the template from stimulus-driven activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate patterns of cortical activity, particularly in frontoparietal attention networks and early visual areas, will contain decodable information about the attended spatial configuration (quadrant vs. ring), even when low-level visual input is held constant.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI with a slow event-related design. Participants will covertly attend to cued locations forming either a quadrant or a ring, detecting targets within these configurations while viewing a uniform, flickering checkerboard. A separate feature-based attention localizer (e.g., attending to motion direction in a separate stimulus block) will provide a model of stimulus-driven responses, which will be regressed out of the main task data. MVPA (using a linear support vector machine, SVM) will then be applied to the residual activity patterns in regions of interest (e.g., intraparietal sulcus, frontal eye fields, V1-V4) to decode the attended configuration.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural evidence for a population-coded representation of an abstract spatial attentional template, dissociated from sensory adaptation. It will clarify the cortical sources of configuration-specific attentional control signals identified in earlier ERP work. The method establishes a robust fMRI paradigm for studying high-level attentional states free of low-level confounds.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining MVPA with a rigorous confound-minimization design (identical stimuli + localizer-based regression) to decode the geometry of the attentional template per se, moving beyond the inferential limitations of adaptation or univariate cue-target paradigms.

proposals.title: Mapping the Spatial Tuning of Attentional Modulation: An fMRI Adaptation Paradigm Inspired by Sonar Signal Attenuation
proposals.motivation: While ERP studies have established the spatial specificity of preparatory attention, the underlying neural population tuning in human cortex remains poorly quantified. Current fMRI methods struggle to measure the sharpness of attentional modulation across space, limiting our understanding of how attended locations are neurally represented. A method to directly measure population-level tuning profiles for attended spatial coordinates is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fMRI signal recovery (release from adaptation) for visual probes will reveal distinct, quantifiable tuning profiles, with sharper tuning (less recovery) for probes near an adapted, attended location. Furthermore, the geometry of these tuning profiles will differ for attention directed to Cartesian (quadrant) versus polar (ring-shaped) regions.
proposals.method: We adapt the logic of sonar beamforming's signal attenuation to an fMRI adaptation paradigm. Participants will covertly attend to a repeating 'adapter' stimulus in one visual quadrant. Following adaptation, brief 'probe' stimuli will be presented at the adapter location and at systematically varied locations in other quadrants and ring positions. The BOLD signal recovery for each probe will be modeled using a general linear model (GLM) with parametric regressors for spatial distance from the adapter. Tuning curves will be constructed by plotting signal recovery against spatial offset, separately for quadrant and ring attention tasks.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct, quantitative maps of population-level spatial tuning for attended locations in human visual cortex. It will empirically test whether attentional modulation follows a gradient or a more discrete boundary, and directly compare neural representations for different spatial coordinate systems. The method offers a novel, powerful tool for studying the neural geometry of spatial attention.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual import of a signal attenuation framework from sonar to cognitive neuroscience, and in its specific application to quantitatively compare Cartesian and polar attentional tuning—a question previously addressed only with behavioral or ERP measures, not population-level fMRI tuning curves.

proposals.title: The Neural Signatures of Global and Hemifield Attention: An ERP Investigation of Attentional Scale
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on visual-spatial attention, including seminal work by Eimer, has extensively mapped ERP components like the N2pc and Pd to the selection of specific locations, quadrants, or hemifields. However, the neural mechanisms underlying a 'default' state of attending diffusely to the entire visual field—a common real-world condition—remain poorly characterized and are often treated as a passive baseline rather than an active attentional state. This study aims to systematically compare active global attention with focused hemifield attention to understand how attentional control scales with the size of the attended region.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that actively attending to the entire visual field will elicit distinct, sustained preparatory ERP modulations over posterior sites, differing in topography and time course from the lateralized components (e.g., ADAN, LDAP) associated with directing attention to a single hemifield.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms. In separate blocks, a central symbolic cue will instruct participants to covertly attend to either the left hemifield, right hemifield, or the entire visual field in preparation for a subsequent target detection task. High-density EEG (e.g., 64-channel) will be recorded. Analysis will focus on cue-target interval ERPs, contrasting global vs. hemifield conditions. We will employ mass-univariate analyses (cluster-based permutation tests) to identify significant time-space clusters of activity, alongside traditional component analyses (mean amplitudes) at predefined electrode sites (e.g., contralateral/ipsilateral to cued hemifield, midline sites for global attention).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct ERP characterization of full-field attentional selection, moving beyond its use as a neutral baseline. It will clarify whether global attention constitutes a unique control state or merely the sum of bilateral focal attentional deployments. The findings will refine models of the attentional control network by specifying the neural resources required for different spatial scales of selection.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have contrasted attended vs. unattended hemifields or smaller regions, systematically comparing an active global attentional state to a focused hemifield state within the same paradigm is novel. This directly tests the scalability of known attentional control mechanisms.

proposals.title: The Neural Dynamics of Attentional Exclusion: ERP Signatures of Selection via Distractor Shape Definition
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on spatial attention, including seminal work by Eimer, has overwhelmingly focused on the neural and behavioral consequences of selecting a specific region or object for processing. However, real-world attention often requires ignoring distracting information, a process less well understood. This proposal inverts the classic paradigm to investigate whether the brain implements distinct preparatory and selective mechanisms when the task is to define and exclude a specific distractor shape, rather than to define an attended target region.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that defining a to-be-ignored region will elicit a qualitatively different pattern of preparatory neural activity (e.g., in the Contingent Negative Variation, CNV) and subsequent target selection effects (e.g., N2pc) compared to defining a to-be-attended region, reflecting an active suppression mechanism rather than a facilitatory selection process.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms for event-related potential (ERP) recording. In separate blocks, visual cues will instruct participants to either attend to a specific shape (e.g., a circle) or ignore a specific shape (e.g., a diamond) within a multi-shape array. The spatial location of targets and distractors will be unpredictable, forcing selection based on the cued shape. We will compare ERP components linked to preparatory attention (lateralized CNV) and selective processing (N2pc) between the 'attend-shape' and 'ignore-shape' conditions. A key analysis will be to examine whether the N2pc, typically a marker of target selection, is reduced, absent, or replaced by a positivity (Pd) when selection is driven by distractor definition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct electrophysiological comparison of target-oriented versus distractor-oriented spatial selection based on shape. It will clarify whether 'ignoring' is merely the absence of attention or an active inhibitory process with its own neural signature. The findings will bridge research on spatial attention and cognitive control, informing models of how the brain configures attentional sets to filter irrelevant information.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined distractor suppression, they typically do so within a paradigm where the *target* location or feature is cued. Our novel approach cues the distractor's defining feature, making the ignored region the primary defined element of the attentional set, which is a fundamental inversion of the standard attentional control problem.

proposals.title: Electrophysiological Signatures of Arbitrary Cueing for Complex Multi-Scale Spatial Attention
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on attentional control, including Eimer's work, distinguishes between endogenous (voluntary) and exogenous (automatic) orienting, often linking automatic capture to feature overlap (e.g., a color cue for a color target). This proposal tests a fundamental limit of learning: can an arbitrary, non-overlapping sensory cue (e.g., a tone) trigger the automatic deployment of a complex, multi-location spatial configuration? This challenges core models of automaticity and attentional control, probing the flexibility of the cognitive architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: Extensive, consistent training can forge a direct, automatic link between an arbitrary auditory or visual feature cue and the deployment of a complex, multi-ring spatial attentional template, as evidenced by rapid, cue-elicited ERP components associated with spatial selection (e.g., early directing attention negativity, EDAN).
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's ring/quadrant ERP paradigms. In a training phase, participants learn that a specific, arbitrary cue (e.g., a high-pitch tone) reliably predicts that subsequent visual targets will appear within two concentric rings, while a different cue (low-pitch tone) predicts a different multi-ring configuration. After extensive training, the test phase will use ERP measures to assess automaticity. We will analyze early visual ERP components (P1, N1) and attention-related negativities (EDAN, ADAN) time-locked to the cue onset under conditions of high perceptual load to determine if the complex spatial template is deployed automatically upon cue presentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide novel evidence on the boundaries of learned automaticity in spatial attention, demonstrating whether complex spatial configurations can become reflexively triggered stimuli. It will refine theoretical models of attentional control by specifying the conditions under which arbitrary cues can gain direct access to the spatial selection system, bridging research on contingent capture with that on preparatory attention templates.
proposals.novelty: While previous contingent capture studies show automaticity for simple features (e.g., color), this tests automaticity for a complex, multi-location spatial *configuration* cued by a dimensionally arbitrary signal, moving beyond simple feature overlap to assess the automatization of a specific attentional control setting.

proposals.title: The Emergence of Automatic Attentional Capture by a Trained Distractor Shape: An ERP Investigation
proposals.motivation: Theories of attention posit that automatic capture is driven by salience or learned target features. However, it is unknown whether a visual shape defined purely as a to-be-ignored distractor region can, through extensive training, become an automatic attentional cue. This challenges the fundamental distinction between goal-directed and stimulus-driven control, probing the plasticity of attentional settings and the potential for automaticity to be anchored to negative templates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that after prolonged associative training, an arbitrary, non-salient shape that consistently defines a distractor region will involuntarily capture spatial attention, as indexed by early lateralized ERP components (N2pc), even when it is task-irrelevant.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's spatial cueing paradigms using ERP measures. Participants will complete a training phase where a specific, arbitrary shape (e.g., an irregular polygon) consistently defines the location of a non-target distractor in a visual search task. In subsequent test blocks, this shape will appear as an uninformative peripheral cue preceding a target in a simple detection or discrimination task. We will measure the N2pc and other lateralized components (ADAN, LDAP) to the cue itself to assess automatic spatial orienting. Control conditions will use novel shapes or shapes associated with targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct electrophysiological evidence for or against the automatic capture of attention by a learned distractor shape. It will clarify the boundary conditions of attentional learning, showing whether automatic orienting can be driven by a 'what-to-ignore' template. The findings will critically inform models of attentional control, such as the Theory of Visual Attention (TVA) and the contingent capture hypothesis.
proposals.novelty: Existing work shows capture by target features or salient singletons; this tests capture by a shape with no initial relevance, defined solely by its negative role as a distractor. It uniquely investigates whether the attentional system can learn to automatically orient *toward* a stimulus that signals where *not* to attend.

proposals.title: Contextual Modulation of Early Motor Preparation: An LRP Study of Probabilistic Task-Set Influences
proposals.motivation: The lateralized readiness potential (LRP) is a key EEG marker for studying motor preparation. Its early phase is often interpreted as a purely automatic, stimulus-driven process, analogous to 'preattentive' visual feature detection. However, pre-2026 research in visual attention has shown that supposedly automatic processes can be modulated by top-down task context, challenging strict dichotomies. It remains unknown if similar contextual modulation applies to early motor cortex activation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the amplitude and latency of the early LRP component, elicited by an identical imperative stimulus, will be systematically modulated by the probabilistic task context (e.g., high vs. low probability of requiring a motor response), demonstrating that it is not purely automatic but sensitive to top-down expectations.
proposals.method: We will adapt established spatial attention and response-priming paradigms from Eimer's work. In a within-subjects design, participants will respond to visual targets (e.g., left/right arrows) presented at cued locations. The critical manipulation is the block-wise probability that a specific, physically identical stimulus (e.g., a green circle) serves as a Go signal (requiring a speeded response) versus a No-Go signal (requiring inhibition). EEG will be recorded, and LRPs will be computed time-locked to this critical stimulus. The early LRP (typically 150-300 ms post-stimulus) will be compared across high-probability Go and high-probability No-Go blocks using standard pre-2026 ERP analysis techniques (e.g., cluster-based permutation tests).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a core assumption in cognitive neuroscience regarding the automaticity of motor preparation. It will refine models of the motor system by delineating the boundary between stimulus-driven and context-sensitive processes. The findings will bridge concepts from visual attention and motor control, offering a more integrated view of cognitive architecture.
proposals.novelty: While LRPs have been studied extensively in inhibition tasks (e.g., stop-signal), the novel approach is to use an identical physical stimulus and manipulate its *probabilistic task role* across blocks, directly analogous to tests of automaticity in vision. This isolates the effect of top-down task-set from low-level stimulus properties.

proposals.title: Causal Dissociation of Attentional and Motor Contributions to the Early Lateralized Readiness Potential: A Concurrent TMS-EEG-fMRI Study
proposals.motivation: The early lateralized readiness potential (LRP) is a key EEG marker of response preparation, but its functional interpretation remains ambiguous. Pre-2026 research cannot determine if it directly reflects covert attentional orienting to a response-relevant location or specific motor preparation. Resolving this is critical for understanding the neural timeline of sensorimotor transformation and for interpreting the LRP in studies of attention and action planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the early LRP is a direct neural correlate of spatial attentional shifting, not motor preparation. Therefore, TMS perturbation of dorsal attention network nodes (e.g., intraparietal sulcus) will selectively modulate the early LRP's amplitude and latency, while perturbation of primary motor cortex will not.
proposals.method: Participants will perform a cued spatial attention task with left/right manual responses. We will employ concurrent EEG-fMRI to acquire the LRP and localize individual target regions for TMS. In separate blocks, precisely timed single-pulse TMS will be delivered to either the contralateral intraparietal sulcus (IPS) or primary motor cortex (M1) at a critical pre-LRP latency (e.g., 100-150 ms post-cue). The effects of site-specific TMS on early LRP metrics will be compared to a no-TMS baseline using repeated-measures ANOVA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for the functional significance of the early LRP, directly testing competing attentional vs. motor accounts. It will establish a novel multimodal perturbation paradigm (TMS-EEG-fMRI) for dissociating preparatory brain activity. The findings will clarify the neural generators of a fundamental ERP component used across cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: While TMS-EEG and EEG-fMRI exist separately, their concurrent application to perturb and measure a specific, time-locked component like the LRP is novel. The adversarial design—pitting attentional against motor node perturbation—moves beyond correlative localization to test necessity, a significant advance over prior ERP source modeling studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying Attentional Activation: A Diffusion Model Analysis of Cue-Induced Response Preparation in Visual Spatial Attention
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on spatial attention, including Martin Eimer's seminal ERP work, has established that cues automatically activate responses to expected target locations. However, traditional measures like reaction times and ERPs provide only indirect, aggregate indices of this preparatory activation. A precise, quantitative model of the underlying evidence accumulation process triggered by the cue is lacking, limiting our mechanistic understanding of attentional control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the automatic response activation elicited by a spatial cue can be formally modeled as a diffusion process, where cue information drives a continuous, noisy accumulation of evidence toward a specific response threshold. The drift rate of this process will be systematically modulated by the spatial specificity of the attentional cue.
proposals.method: We will apply the hierarchical drift-diffusion model (HDDM) to behavioral data from a classic spatial cueing paradigm. Participants will respond to visual targets appearing at cued or uncued locations. The model will fit trial-by-trial reaction time distributions for correct and error responses, estimating key parameters: drift rate (reflecting cue-induced evidence strength), boundary separation (response caution), and non-decision time. Model comparisons will test if drift rates are higher for validly versus invalidly cued trials and for spatially precise (single location) versus broad (quadrant/ring) cues.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, quantitative link between cognitive theories of attentional preparation and sequential sampling models of decision-making. It will yield a novel computational metric for the strength of automatic response activation, moving beyond mean RT differences. The findings will refine models of attentional control by specifying how spatial information is transformed into motor preparation.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are standard in perceptual decision tasks, their application to model the preparatory, cue-induced activation phase preceding target onset in spatial attention is novel. This integrates two previously separate literatures: cognitive neuroscience of attention and computational modeling of decision dynamics.

proposals.title: Dynamic Network Reconfiguration of Early Visual Cortex from Controlled to Automatic Processing: An fMRI Study of Perceptual Expertise
proposals.motivation: Perceptual expertise fundamentally alters stimulus processing, but its neural basis is debated. While training-induced plasticity in early visual cortex (EVC) is established, its functional role remains ambiguous—is it enhanced sensory analysis or a locus of automated post-perceptual decision codes? This proposal addresses this by examining how EVC's large-scale network affiliations reconfigure with expertise, moving beyond local activation changes to understand its shifting computational role within the brain's functional architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extensive perceptual training on complex shapes will induce a shift in the functional connectivity of EVC, from strong coupling with fronto-parietal control networks during novice, effortful processing to increased integration with ventral stream object recognition and default-mode networks during expert, automatic processing.
proposals.method: We will train participants over weeks on a demanding shape discrimination task. Using fMRI, we will acquire resting-state and task-state data at multiple timepoints. Functional connectivity will be analyzed using seed-based correlation and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses, with seeds in retinotopically defined EVC areas (V1-V3). Graph-theoretic measures (e.g., participation coefficient) will quantify shifts in EVC's network modularity. Multivoxel pattern analysis (MVPA) will track the representational content of EVC activity patterns across training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel network-level account of perceptual expertise, demonstrating that EVC's functional definition is dynamic and context-dependent. It will bridge literatures on perceptual learning, cognitive control, and brain network dynamics, offering a mechanistic framework for how controlled processing becomes automatic through cortical reorganization.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the traditional view by treating EVC not as a fixed input stage but as a plastic network node whose functional role is defined by its dynamic connectivity, a perspective not yet integrated into models of attentional control and expertise where Eimer's work on spatial selection is central.

proposals.title: Automaticity of Spatial Attention: ERP Evidence from Subliminal and Metacontrast-Masked Cues
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Eimer and others has established that spatial attention can be deployed voluntarily (endogenously) or triggered by salient cues (exogenously). However, the boundary conditions for truly automatic, stimulus-driven orienting remain unclear. Testing with subtle, near-threshold cues can dissociate automatic activation from conscious control, addressing a core debate in attention research about the necessity of awareness for attentional shifts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that even subliminal spatial cues and metacontrast-masked primes will elicit early, lateralized ERP components (e.g., N2pc, ADAN), indicating automatic, unconscious shifts of spatial attention, but that these effects will be transient and distinct from those elicited by supraliminal cues.
proposals.method: We will adapt Eimer's established spatial cueing paradigms for visual and crossmodal (visual-tactile) tasks. Cues will be rendered subliminal via brief presentation and pattern masking, or masked using metacontrast. High-density EEG (64-channel) will be recorded. The primary analysis will focus on established lateralized ERP components (ADAN, LDAP, N2pc) as markers of attentional orienting. Signal detection theory will be used to verify cue invisibility at the individual participant level, and effects will be compared across subliminal, masked, and supraliminal cue conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct electrophysiological evidence for or against the automaticity of spatial attention in the absence of awareness. It will clarify the functional role of early ERP components by testing their sensitivity to unconscious information. The findings will refine theoretical models of attentional control, specifying the conditions under which spatial selection is truly obligatory.
proposals.novelty: While subliminal priming is well-studied for object recognition, its application to the specific domain of *spatial* attention, using the precise ERP markers developed in Eimer's lab, is novel. Combining metacontrast masking with crossmodal spatial attention paradigms to probe automaticity across senses is a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Single-neuron correlates of preparatory spatial attention in the macaque frontoparietal network during a crossmodal cueing task
proposals.motivation: Human ERP studies by Eimer and others have robustly characterized the spatiotemporal dynamics of preparatory spatial attention, revealing distinct components like the ADAN and LDAP. However, the neural generators and cellular-level mechanisms underlying these preparatory states, especially their crossmodal control, remain poorly understood. Animal neurophysiology is essential to bridge this gap, moving from scalp-level correlates to the activity of specific neuronal populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neurons in macaque frontal eye fields (FEF) and lateral intraparietal area (LIP) will exhibit spatially specific, modality-independent preparatory activity following a spatial cue, and that the strength of this activity will predict behavioral performance on the subsequent target detection task.
proposals.method: We will train two macaque monkeys on a spatial cueing task adapted from Eimer's human paradigms, using central auditory or tactile cues to direct attention to a left or right visual hemifield. We will chronically implant multi-electrode arrays in FEF and LIP to record single-unit and multi-unit activity. Neural data will be aligned to cue onset and analyzed for spatially selective preparatory activity using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis and generalized linear models (GLMs) to dissect contributions of cue modality, attended location, and expected target features.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, cellular-level description of preparatory attentional states in a primate frontoparietal network during a validated crossmodal cueing paradigm. It will identify candidate neural generators for human ERP components and test whether attentional control signals are abstract (supramodal) or modality-specific at the single-neuron level.
proposals.novelty: While primate neurophysiology has studied spatial attention, it has rarely employed the precise, crossmodal cueing designs central to human cognitive neuroscience of attention. Applying Eimer's well-controlled paradigms to animal models creates a direct bridge between human ERP markers and underlying cellular mechanisms.

ideas.title: Invert the causality of attention
ideas.description: Explore if changes in sensory precision can cause, rather than result from, attentional deployment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Preparatory states in crossmodal spatial attention: spatial specificity and possible control mechanisms

ideas.title: Dual: study auditory spatial attention
ideas.description: Apply the sequential location method to the auditory domain using speakers arranged in space.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Invert the probability manipulation of cue validity
ideas.description: Make opposite-location trials the frequent, valid event to see if the ERP effects reverse or change pattern.
ideas.sourceTitle: An ERP study on visual spatial priming with peripheral onsets.

ideas.title: Invert the cue-target modality relationship
ideas.description: Use cues in one modality to direct attention to targets in a different, unpredictable modality.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP effects of intermodal attention and cross-modal links in spatial attention.

ideas.title: Invert the role of expectation
ideas.description: Make the unexpected location the target, testing if inhibition, not facilitation, drives the effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Causal dissociation of attentional and motor contributions to the early LRP using concurrent TMS-EEG-fMRI
ideas.description: This project aims to causally determine whether the early lateralized readiness potential (LRP) directly reflects attentional shifting or motor preparation. We will employ a multimodal neuroimaging and perturbation design, integrating simultaneous EEG (to measure the LRP), fMRI (to localize activated networks), and precisely timed, single-pulse TMS to disrupt candidate brain regions. By applying TMS to attentional versus motor nodes at critical pre-LRP latencies during a cued spatial attention task, we can test which perturbation selectively modulates the early LRP's amplitude and latency. This causal interference approach moves beyond correlation to identify the necessary neural substrate for the early LRP, resolving its functional interpretation.

ideas.title: Investigate the inverse: priming with target as cue
ideas.description: Reverse the sequence, using the 'target' stimulus as a cue for a subsequent probe to examine backward priming.
ideas.sourceTitle: An ERP study on visual spatial priming with peripheral onsets.

ideas.title: Testing the 'Reflex' Assumption: Is Early Motor Cortex Activation Truly Context-Immune?
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that early LRP activation is purely automatic and stimulus-driven by drawing an analogy from cognitive psychology's investigation of 'preattentive' visual processing. In vision research, the purported automaticity of early feature detection was tested by embedding identical stimuli in different task contexts (e.g., as a target vs. a distractor) to see if processing was modulated. The analogous solution is adapted by designing a motor response task where the identical imperative stimulus appears with different task probabilities across blocks (e.g., high probability of requiring a response vs. high probability of requiring inhibition). By comparing the early LRP—a marker of motor cortex preparation—between these contexts, the research can directly test if this early motor activation is truly immune to task context or is instead influenced by top-down probabilistic expectations, thereby refining models of automaticity in the motor system.

ideas.title: Automatic Attentional Capture by Arbitrary Distractor Shapes
ideas.description: This research investigates whether extensive training can lead to automatic attentional capture by a visual shape that is defined purely as the to-be-ignored region, not the target. The core question is whether an arbitrary, non-salient shape, which initially holds no dimensional overlap with the task, can become an automatic cue that involuntarily draws attention after prolonged associative training. This directly challenges traditional theories by testing if automaticity can be anchored to a negatively defined 'distractor template' rather than a target feature, probing the fundamental plasticity and boundary conditions of attentional control.

ideas.title: Investigate the developmental trajectory
ideas.description: Study how cross-modal links in spatial attention emerge and change from infancy through old age.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP effects of intermodal attention and cross-modal links in spatial attention.

ideas.title: Apply fMRI to map attention networks
ideas.description: Use fMRI's spatial resolution to visualize the brain networks involved in intermodal and cross-modal spatial attention.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP effects of intermodal attention and cross-modal links in spatial attention.

ideas.title: Use computational modeling of attention
ideas.description: Apply neural network models to simulate the asymmetric cross-modal gating observed in ERP data.
ideas.sourceTitle: An event-related brain potential study of cross-modal links in spatial attention between vision and touch.

ideas.title: Multivariate decoding of attentional templates for spatial configurations
ideas.description: This improved study uses fMRI with multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to directly decode the spatial configuration of covert attention (quadrants vs. rings) from distributed cortical activity patterns. Identical, flickering checkerboard stimuli are presented across all conditions, while participants perform a detection task at cued locations, eliminating low-level visual confounds. The design includes a feature-based attention localizer to model and regress out stimulus-driven responses, isolating the template for the attended spatial region. This approach robustly measures the population coding of the attentional template itself, rather than relying on the ambiguous univariate adaptation signal.

ideas.title: Test cross-modal links under divided attention
ideas.description: Examine if effects persist when attention is split between locations or modalities.
ideas.sourceTitle: An event-related brain potential study of cross-modal links in spatial attention between vision and touch.

ideas.title: Examine the inverse: attention decoupling
ideas.description: Create conditions that force attention to decouple across modalities and measure the behavioral and neural cost.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP effects of intermodal attention and cross-modal links in spatial attention.

ideas.title: Adapting Acoustic Filter Design to Visual ERP Configurations
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from signal processing in acoustics, where complex sounds are decomposed using filter banks to analyze their frequency components. The analogous solution involves isolating specific, sometimes non-contiguous, frequency bands for individual study. Adapted to visual ERP research, the proposed method is to investigate attention to complex spatial configurations (like annuli or wedges) not just as holistic shapes, but by designing experimental paradigms that functionally act as a 'visual spatial filter bank.' This would involve creating stimuli and attention tasks that isolate the contributions of simpler elements (e.g., curvature, radial boundaries, gaps) that constitute the complex shape. By measuring ERPs for both the complex shape and its decomposed primitives, we can build a model to determine if the neural signature of attending to the complex configuration is a simple linear sum or involves more complex, non-linear integration of these spatial features.

ideas.title: Use functional MRI for spatial localization
ideas.description: Employ fMRI to identify the broader network of brain regions involved in spatial priming and inhibition.
ideas.sourceTitle: An ERP study on visual spatial priming with peripheral onsets.

ideas.title: Vary cue-target onset asynchrony systematically
ideas.description: Manipulate the time interval between cue and target to trace the time course of facilitation and inhibition.
ideas.sourceTitle: An ERP study on visual spatial priming with peripheral onsets.

ideas.title: Compare sequential cues to traditional precues
ideas.description: Directly pit the sequential location method against classic peripheral or central precuing in the same experiment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Scale up to full-field selection
ideas.description: Compare ERP effects for attending to the entire visual field versus a single hemifield.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attending to quadrants and ring-shaped regions: ERP effects of visual attention in different spatial selection tasks.

ideas.title: Model inattention and neglect
ideas.description: Apply the framework to pathological deficits of attention, like spatial neglect.
ideas.sourceTitle: Preparatory states in crossmodal spatial attention: spatial specificity and possible control mechanisms

ideas.title: Challenge that transient and sustained are distinct
ideas.description: Test if sustained attention is just a series of transient shifts, not a separate mechanism.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP modulations indicate the selective processing of visual stimuli as a result of transient and sustained spatial attention.

ideas.title: Relax the assumption of pure symbolic cues
ideas.description: Use cues that are both symbolic and spatially predictive to see interactive effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP modulations indicate the selective processing of visual stimuli as a result of transient and sustained spatial attention.

ideas.title: Challenge the separability of identity and location tasks
ideas.description: Design a task requiring integrated processing of identity and location to see if priming effects interact.
ideas.sourceTitle: An ERP study on visual spatial priming with peripheral onsets.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Spatial and Feature-Based Selection in Ring Attention
ideas.description: This research creates a synthetic benchmark to causally test the selection mechanism of ring attention. We generate visual scenes where target objects with specific curvature features appear at randomized locations, independent of distractor features and locations. By training ring attention models on this benchmark and using ablation studies that selectively mask spatial or feature information, we can quantitatively measure the contribution of each selection pathway. This allows for a definitive conclusion on whether selection is purely spatial, purely feature-based, or a hybrid, overcoming the confounds of natural image statistics.

ideas.title: Automaticity of Complex Spatial Configurations via Arbitrary Cueing
ideas.description: This research investigates whether automatic, rapid processing of complex visual configurations—specifically, the simultaneous attention to multiple concentric or disjoint rings—can be elicited by arbitrary sensory cues (e.g., a tone or color) that share no dimensional overlap with the target structure. It challenges the traditional view that automaticity requires feature overlap by testing if extensive, consistent training can forge a direct, automatic link between an arbitrary cue and a demanding multi-scale spatial analysis task. The core question is whether the cognitive system can automatize not just a simple feature detection, but the deployment of a specific, complex attentional template, based purely on learned association.

ideas.title: Apply transcranial magnetic stimulation
ideas.description: Use TMS to disrupt specific cortical areas and test causal role in cross-modal attention.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP effects of intermodal attention and cross-modal links in spatial attention.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: decode focus from EEG
ideas.description: Use machine learning to decode the likely current focus of attention from the EEG signal alone.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Apply transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS)
ideas.description: Use TMS to disrupt specific cortical areas and test necessity for cross-modal ERP effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: An event-related brain potential study of cross-modal links in spatial attention between vision and touch.

ideas.title: Use animal neurophysiology to study cellular mechanisms
ideas.description: Record from single neurons in animal models during a similar spatial cueing task.
ideas.sourceTitle: Spatial cueing, sensory gating and selective response preparation: an ERP study on visuo-spatial orienting.

ideas.title: Test object-grouping as a perceptual 'Gestalt' for quadrant selection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that quadrant selection operates primarily via a spatial zoom-lens mechanism. By drawing an analogy to Gestalt Psychology, where perceptual grouping principles (like proximity, similarity, and common fate) explain object-based organization in vision, we propose testing if similar object-based grouping is the primary mechanism for quadrant selection. The adapted research idea involves designing experiments that pit spatial cues against object-based Gestalt cues to see which dominantly drives selection, potentially revealing that the visual system uses object-grouping heuristics rather than a simple spatial window for attentional selection.

ideas.title: Extend to non-spatial feature attention
ideas.description: Apply the free-energy framework to model attention directed to color, motion, or object features.
ideas.sourceTitle: Preparatory states in crossmodal spatial attention: spatial specificity and possible control mechanisms

ideas.title: Individual Differences in the Trainability of Early Sensory Filters for Complex Shape Processing
ideas.description: This research program investigates the relationship between individual differences in automatic response activation and the capacity for perceptual learning in early sensory cortices. It tests the hypothesis that individuals with stronger baseline automatic activation may show greater or more rapid training-induced modulations in early visual processing of complex shapes. The study would employ longitudinal training on complex shape discrimination combined with neuroimaging (e.g., fMRI or EEG) to track changes in early visual areas, while also measuring individual differences in automatic activation using behavioral tasks like the flanker or Simon task. This approach seeks to determine if a core cognitive trait predicts the plasticity of supposedly rigid sensory processing stages, offering a unified account of individual variability in both automatic processing and perceptual expertise.

ideas.title: Study attentional momentum across trials
ideas.description: Investigate how the direction of previous shifts builds momentum affecting performance over longer sequences.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Apply fMRI adaptation to spatial attention using a signal attenuation approach from sonar
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from underwater acoustics and sonar signal processing to the fMRI study of spatial attention. In sonar, adaptive beamforming techniques measure how a sensor array's response attenuates to a repeated signal and recovers for signals from new directions, mapping directional sensitivity. Applying this analogically, we design an fMRI adaptation paradigm where a visual 'adapter' stimulus is repeatedly presented in one attended spatial quadrant, inducing neural adaptation. Following this, 'probe' stimuli are presented in various locations, including the same quadrant, different quadrants, and ring positions. The key measurement is the degree of fMRI signal recovery (release from adaptation) for each probe location, which serves as a direct metric of the tuning sharpness and selectivity of the attention-modulated neural population. This allows us to quantitatively compare neural population tuning profiles for attended locations defined by Cartesian (quadrant) versus polar (ring) coordinates, providing a novel method to dissect the neural representation of attended space.

ideas.title: Investigate neural generators of midline negativity
ideas.description: Use source localization to identify brain areas producing the midline negativity for valid trials.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP modulations indicate the selective processing of visual stimuli as a result of transient and sustained spatial attention.

ideas.title: Invert the sensory vs postperceptual dichotomy
ideas.description: Investigate if early sensory modulations can be trained for complex shapes with extensive practice.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attending to quadrants and ring-shaped regions: ERP effects of visual attention in different spatial selection tasks.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-stimulus features
ideas.description: Test automatic activation with subtle, barely perceptible spatial cues or masked primes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stimulus-response compatibility and automatic response activation: evidence from psychophysiological studies.

ideas.title: Apply psychophysical reverse correlation techniques
ideas.description: Use reverse correlation to map the attentional receptive field shaped by the sequential stimulus history.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Use continuous flash suppression from psychophysics
ideas.description: Combine the sequential method with interocular suppression to probe attention's role in unconscious processing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Solve the inverse attention problem
ideas.description: Infer an observer's internal model and precision beliefs from their behavioral and neural data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Preparatory states in crossmodal spatial attention: spatial specificity and possible control mechanisms

ideas.title: Challenge visual meridian as special boundary
ideas.description: Test if meridian effects are due to cortical architecture or task-specific strategic factors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Dual-task interference on neural correlates
ideas.description: Add a concurrent secondary task to determine which ERP components of sequence learning are resource-dependent.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit and implicit learning of event sequences: evidence from event-related brain potentials.

ideas.title: Assume non-hierarchical perception
ideas.description: Test if attentional phenomena emerge in flat, non-hierarchical probabilistic models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Preparatory states in crossmodal spatial attention: spatial specificity and possible control mechanisms

ideas.title: Relax assumption of discrete attentional focus
ideas.description: Investigate if the 'focus' is a single point or a distributed field using high-density spatial sampling.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Mapping the Cortical Reorganization of Sensory Networks from Controlled to Automatic Processing via Perceptual Expertise
ideas.description: This research program uses fMRI to track how extensive perceptual training on complex shapes alters the functional role of early visual cortex. The core hypothesis is that training-induced modulations in early sensory areas will be accompanied by a shift in their network affiliations, moving from being coupled with fronto-parietal control networks during effortful, controlled processing to becoming integrated into more automatic, streamlined processing networks. This approach inverts the traditional dichotomy by treating early sensory cortex not as a fixed input stage, but as a plastic component whose functional definition (sensory vs. post-perceptual) depends on its dynamic network embedding, which changes with expertise.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Distracter shape definition
ideas.description: Study selection by defining the to-be-ignored region shape instead of the attended region.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attending to quadrants and ring-shaped regions: ERP effects of visual attention in different spatial selection tasks.

ideas.title: Apply diffusion tensor imaging (DTI)
ideas.description: Map white matter connectivity between visual and somatosensory cortices to predict individual ERP effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: An event-related brain potential study of cross-modal links in spatial attention between vision and touch.

ideas.title: Apply diffusion modeling to response activation
ideas.description: Use sequential sampling models to quantify evidence accumulation from the cue's automatic activation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stimulus-response compatibility and automatic response activation: evidence from psychophysiological studies.

ideas.title: Invert the cue validity effect assumption
ideas.description: Test if invalid cues sometimes provide a benefit by promoting broader perceptual monitoring.
ideas.sourceTitle: ERP modulations indicate the selective processing of visual stimuli as a result of transient and sustained spatial attention.

ideas.title: Early activation is purely stimulus-driven
ideas.description: Test if the early LRP activation is truly automatic or influenced by task context.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stimulus-response compatibility and automatic response activation: evidence from psychophysiological studies.

ideas.title: Investigate neural generators of PI enhancement
ideas.description: Use source localization methods to identify the brain regions responsible for the PI enhancement to opposite-location targets.
ideas.sourceTitle: An ERP study on visual spatial priming with peripheral onsets.

ideas.title: ERP correlates of implicit-only learning
ideas.description: Study the N2 and LRP components in participants who show no explicit knowledge to isolate pure implicit learning signatures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit and implicit learning of event sequences: evidence from event-related brain potentials.

ideas.title: Causal dissection of controlled activation components via factorial manipulation and high-resolution chronometry
ideas.description: This study moves beyond descriptive chronometry to causally identify the sub-processes within the controlled activation phase. We employ a factorial design that independently manipulates demands on conflict monitoring, inhibitory control, and selective response activation within the same task, while recording high-density EEG. By analyzing how the timing and amplitude of specific neural components (e.g., the N2, P3) are selectively modulated by each experimental factor, we can map distinct control operations onto precise time windows and neural generators, moving from correlation to a functional decomposition of the controlled phase.

ideas.title: Relax assumption of unimodal target detection
ideas.description: Study cross-modal links when targets are defined by bimodal (visuotactile) feature conjunctions.
ideas.sourceTitle: An event-related brain potential study of cross-modal links in spatial attention between vision and touch.

ideas.title: Use animal neurophysiology methods
ideas.description: Employ single-unit recording in multisensory cortical areas during a similar cross-modal attention task.
ideas.sourceTitle: An event-related brain potential study of cross-modal links in spatial attention between vision and touch.

ideas.title: Eye-Tracking-Guided fMRI Adaptation to Decode Fixation-Locked Attentional Tuning
ideas.description: This study integrates high-precision eye-tracking with fMRI adaptation to investigate the neural tuning of spatial attention under rigorously controlled fixation. Eye-tracking ensures central fixation and provides trial-by-trial metrics of microsaccades and fixation stability, which are used to categorize and gate the fMRI adaptation paradigm. By applying adaptation protocols specifically during periods of stable fixation versus microsaccade occurrence, we can directly measure how these distinct oculomotor states differentially affect neural population tuning for attended quadrants and rings in visual and parietal cortex. This creates a novel causal link between the precise oculomotor signature of an attentional strategy and its corresponding neural tuning profile.

ideas.title: Challenge necessity of predictable sequences
ideas.description: Test if attentional gradients form with probabilistic, not perfectly predictable, sequential stimulus orders.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Causal network dissection of spatial selection strategies via combined MEG-TMS and computational modeling
ideas.description: This integrated approach combines the millisecond temporal resolution of MEG with the causal intervention of online repetitive TMS and computational modeling to dissect the neural mechanisms of quadrant versus ring selection. After using MEG to generate hypotheses about the timing and nodes of the two selection processes, TMS is applied at those specific times and locations to test their necessary role by measuring selective behavioral disruption. The causal outcomes from TMS are then used to constrain and validate a Dynamic Causal Model (DCM) of the effective connectivity between the involved cortical regions, moving beyond mere correlation to a testable model of the network dynamics underlying the two spatial strategies.

ideas.title: Assume attention shifts are not obligatory
ideas.description: Test if observers can resist predictive shifts, maintaining a diffuse or fixed distribution.
ideas.sourceTitle: Attentional selection and attentional gradients: an alternative method for studying transient visual-spatial attention.

ideas.title: Scale down to microstimulation of skin
ideas.description: Use precise, graded tactile stimulation to titrate the strength of touch-to-vision attentional modulation.
ideas.sourceTitle: An event-related brain potential study of cross-modal links in spatial attention between vision and touch.

ideas.title: Conditioned Automaticity: Arbitrary Stimulus-Response Binding Through Hebbian Overlearning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that automatic cognitive activation requires dimensional overlap (e.g., semantic, perceptual) between the cue and the response. Drawing an analogy from behavioral neuroscience, specifically classical conditioning where arbitrary stimuli come to elicit conditioned responses through predictive pairing, we propose that automaticity can be synthetically created for arbitrary cues via extensive, overlearned association. The adapted research idea is to conduct a long-term training study where participants repeatedly pair a completely arbitrary, neutral stimulus with a specific cognitive or motor response. After this extensive Hebbian learning phase, we test for automatic activation by embedding the trained cue in a task where it is irrelevant or distracting. If the trained cue now produces classic signatures of automatic processing (e.g., Stroop-like interference, negative priming, or facilitation), it would demonstrate that automaticity is a product of associative strength and not contingent on inherent dimensional overlap, fundamentally reframing our understanding of automatic cognitive architecture.

ideas.title: Individual Differences in the Automatic Processing of Multi-Ring Spatial Configurations
ideas.description: This research investigates how individual differences in the strength of automatic response activation manifest when processing complex visual configurations, specifically multiple concentric or disjoint rings. By systematically scaling the number and spatial relationships of rings, the study creates a parametric task where automatic capture or interference can be measured against an objective metric of visual complexity. This approach allows for the identification of a trait-like capacity for parallel pre-attentive processing, determining whether individuals with stronger automatic activation are more susceptible to interference from complex configurations or, conversely, possess a superior ability to automatically parse them.

ideas.title: Model-based mapping of perceptual and postperceptual selection dynamics using concurrent EEG-fMRI and computational modeling
ideas.description: This study uses concurrent high-density EEG and fMRI to dissect the neural dynamics of selection as a function of shape complexity and task demands. Instead of seeking a single transition point, we fit and compare computational models representing discrete-stage, graded, and parallel processing accounts of selection. The integrated neural data will reveal whether early sensory modulations diminish abruptly, scale continuously, or are maintained via feedback, providing a robust characterization of the selection architecture across the visual hierarchy.

ideas.title: Scale up to multiple, distributed cue locations
ideas.description: Expand the paradigm from two hemifields to many possible cue and target locations in a circle.
ideas.sourceTitle: Spatial cueing, sensory gating and selective response preparation: an ERP study on visuo-spatial orienting.

ideas.title: Neural Decoding of the Temporal Dynamics of Automatic and Controlled Processing Integration During Cognitive Conflict
ideas.description: This study precisely maps how automatic and controlled processes integrate over time by using a cued conflict task (e.g., a Stroop variant where a pre-cue probabilistically indicates the relevant task dimension). High-density EEG will be combined with multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode the time-course of automatic (e.g., word meaning) and controlled (e.g., instructed color) neural representations on a millisecond scale. This allows for testing specific computational models of integration (e.g., early competition followed by controlled suppression, or continuous weighted combination) against the neural data, moving beyond vague descriptions to a formal, testable account of the interaction.

ideas.title: Neurally Constrained Arbitrary Automaticity: Controlling for Latent Dimensional Overlap in Extensive Training
ideas.description: This research program rigorously tests whether automatic activation can be driven by extensive training alone, independent of any dimensional overlap between cue and response. It first uses modeling and adaptive training regimes to detect and disrupt the incidental emergence of latent relational structures during learning. The core test then employs neural decoding to measure automatic activation in brain regions not involved in the original task, providing a purer measure of automaticity. This combined behavioral and neural approach aims to conclusively separate the effects of sheer practice from those of unavoidable associative overlap.

ideas.title: Scale down to microsaccade-locked analysis
ideas.description: Analyze ERPs time-locked to microsaccades to control for their confounding effects on cueing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Spatial cueing, sensory gating and selective response preparation: an ERP study on visuo-spatial orienting.

ideas.title: Scale temporal resolution with intracranial recordings
ideas.description: Use intracranial EEG in epilepsy patients to study spatial priming with millisecond precision in cortical layers.

ideas.title: Inverse Attention via Topological Suppression
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from computational topology to the inverse attention problem. In mesh generation, defining a 'hole' prompts the algorithm to optimally tessellate the surrounding space. Similarly, in this research, we study selection by pre-defining the to-be-ignored region's shape (the 'distracter') rather than the attended region. The analogous solution is adapted by training an attention mechanism to treat this defined distracter shape as a topological void it must suppress, thereby forcing it to adaptively and efficiently scale its focus to the complementary 'non-void' region. This scaling dual approach examines how the properties of the ignored region dictate the structure and resource allocation of attention in the remaining field.

ideas.title: A Convergent Psychophysiological Index of Cognitive Control: Disentangling Suppression-Specific Effort via Triangulated Pupillometry, EEG, and Computational Modeling
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-method framework to isolate the cognitive effort specifically attributable to the controlled suppression of automatic responses. Pupillometry is used to index global task engagement and effort, while simultaneously recorded EEG provides millisecond-resolution neural markers of conflict monitoring (N2) and inhibitory control (P3). These neural and pupillometric signals are then analyzed in the context of trial-by-trial parameters from a drift-diffusion model fitted to behavioral data, which accounts for variance due to perceptual encoding and motor execution. This convergent design statistically isolates the unique pupillary component that correlates with the neural signature of suppression, creating a robust, process-pure index of cognitive control effort.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Array Analysis to MEG for Cortical Timing Localization
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from geophysics to the neuroscience context. In seismology, arrays of sensors use precise timing differences in wave arrivals to localize earthquake sources in space and time. The analogous solution is to apply similar array processing techniques—specifically, advanced beamforming and back-projection algorithms used for seismic source localization—to MEG data. The adapted research idea is to use these methods to treat the MEG sensor array as a unified system for triangulating not only the cortical sources of neural activity but, critically, the millisecond-level timing of their engagement. This would allow for a direct, model-informed comparison of the activation sequences underlying different visual selection processes (quadrant vs. ring), providing a robust method to localize both the 'where' and the precise 'when' of these cognitive operations.
