ideas.title: A Latent-Trait, Multi-Method Investigation of Individual Differences in Automatic Response Activation
ideas.description: This research program aims to rigorously test whether a stable, domain-general trait of automatic response activation strength exists across individuals. It employs a battery of diverse laboratory tasks designed to tap automatic processes in cognitive, affective, and motor domains, while also measuring executive control to disentangle it from automatic tendencies. Using structural equation modeling, the shared variance across these tasks is modeled as a latent factor representing core automaticity, separating it from task-specific artifacts and controlled processing. This design allows for a definitive test of the trait's existence and an examination of its unique correlations with real-world outcomes, personality, and neurophysiological markers.

ideas.title: Closed-loop EEG-triggered TMS for phase-locked disruption of motor cortical dynamics
ideas.description: This system uses real-time EEG to decode the onset of motor preparation (e.g., the Bereitschaftspotential) on a single-trial basis. A closed-loop controller uses this neural trigger to deliver a TMS pulse at a precisely timed delay, targeting either early pre-movement or late execution-related cortical phases. This approach ensures the stimulation is locked to the subject's actual neural events, not just an average or external cue. The system thereby enables rigorous, trial-by-trial investigation of causal contributions from specific temporal phases of the motor cortical activation sequence.

ideas.title: Latent Evidence Diffusion with Explicit Decision Boundaries for Response Activation Modeling
ideas.description: This framework models response activation through a two-stage process that maintains theoretical distinction between evidence dynamics and decision formation. First, a conditional diffusion model generates time-evolving latent representations of evidence states influenced by cues, capturing the automatic activation dynamics. Second, these latent trajectories are processed by an explicit sequential sampling decision module with mathematically defined boundaries, ensuring the decision mechanism remains interpretable within evidence accumulation theory. The system enables joint optimization where the diffusion process learns to generate biologically plausible evidence dynamics while the decision module maps these to observed response patterns, validated through both behavioral and neural data compatibility tests.

ideas.title: Modeling Individual Differences in Automatic Activation with Hierarchical Diffusion Models
ideas.description: This research integrates diffusion modeling with the study of individual differences to create a hierarchical framework for analyzing automatic response activation. The approach uses sequential sampling models to quantify the evidence accumulation process driven by a cue's automatic activation on each trial, while simultaneously estimating population-level distributions and individual-level parameters that capture stable traits in activation strength. This allows researchers to test whether individuals with stronger trait-level automatic biases show fundamentally different evidence accumulation dynamics, such as higher drift rates or lower decision thresholds, in conflict tasks. The resulting model provides a unified account that links latent cognitive processes to observable behavior, predicting how individual differences in automatic activation manifest in specific components of the decision-making architecture.

ideas.title: Use simultaneous EEG-fMRI to temporally dissect the dynamics of automatic versus controlled processing networks
ideas.description: This research employs simultaneous EEG-fMRI to overcome the temporal limitations of fMRI alone. The high-temporal-resolution EEG component is used to identify precise electrophysiological markers (e.g., specific ERPs or spectral power changes) of automatic and controlled processes during a task. These markers then provide precise temporal regressors for the fMRI analysis, allowing the BOLD signal to be decomposed into distinct, sequentially activated networks. This approach yields a spatiotemporally precise model of how automatic and controlled brain systems interact in real time.

ideas.title: Quantifying Individual Differences in Automatic Activation Using Psychometric and Psychophysiological Trait Batteries
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from psychopharmacology, where individual differences in drug sensitivity are mapped using multi-method trait batteries. The analogous problem of measuring individual differences in automatic cognitive/affective activation is solved by not relying on a single behavioral task. Instead, the adapted idea proposes to develop a comprehensive assessment battery. This battery would integrate psychometric self-reports of relevant personality and cognitive styles with performance on a curated set of cognitive psychology tasks designed to elicit automatic responses (e.g., Stroop, priming paradigms). Crucially, these tasks would be administered while collecting concurrent psychophysiological measures (e.g., EEG indices of conflict monitoring like the N2, or pupillary responses). By subjecting this multi-modal dataset to advanced statistical techniques like factor analysis, the research aims to identify and quantify stable, latent traits that represent an individual's characteristic strength of automatic response activation, moving beyond noisy single-task measures.

ideas.title: Use Blind Source Separation (BSS) from Geophysics to Isolate fMRI Activation Sources
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to the neuroscience context. In seismology, Blind Source Separation (BSS) techniques like Independent Component Analysis are used to disentangle mixed signals from multiple independent seismic sources recorded by an array of sensors. By applying these same computational methods to fMRI data, we can statistically separate the mixed BOLD signals originating from distinct, concurrently active brain networks—such as those underlying automatic versus controlled cognitive processes. This provides a powerful, data-driven methodological transfer to localize and characterize the contributing neural activation sources without strong a priori models of the expected hemodynamic responses.

ideas.title: Disentangling Contiguity from Topology in Shape Complexity
ideas.description: This experiment tests whether the perceived complexity of ring-like shapes stems from their lack of visual contiguity or the specific topological property of having a hole. Stimuli are generated as a morphing series from a solid disk to a ring, all with identical outer diameter and total area. Intermediate shapes are 'spoked wheels' with increasing numbers of radial gaps, allowing contiguity to be varied independently of introducing a central hole. By measuring behavioral or neural responses to this continuum, the design isolates the specific contribution of fragmented contour integration versus holistic topological classification to complexity effects.

ideas.title: Adaptive Eye-Tracking Control via Real-Time Biofeedback, Inspired by Flight Simulator Stabilization
ideas.description: This idea expands the original method by incorporating an adaptive control loop inspired by solutions to pilot-induced oscillations in aerospace engineering. The analogous problem of unstable, involuntary movements destabilizing a system is solved with high-frequency sensors and real-time corrective feedback. Adapted to eye-tracking research, we propose a system that uses high-precision eye-tracking not only to ensure central fixation and measure microsaccades but also to implement a real-time biofeedback mechanism. When the system detects deviations predictive of a fixation break or attention-related microsaccade patterns, it delivers an immediate sensory cue. This active intervention trains the participant to maintain optimal fixation, transforming measurement into an interactive control and training paradigm for studying attention.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Sparse Attention with Relational Objectives for Multi-Ring Structures
ideas.description: This research investigates a novel attention architecture designed to efficiently scale complexity across multiple concentric or disjoint rings. The system employs a hierarchical, coarse-to-fine processing strategy with sparsity constraints to limit attention to the most relevant intra-ring and inter-ring interactions. An auxiliary relational objective is integrated to explicitly learn the geometric or functional dependencies between different rings, providing a necessary inductive bias. This approach ensures robust scaling, improved parameter efficiency, and better generalization compared to naive simultaneous attention to all ring elements.

ideas.title: Modeling the Interaction of Automatic and Controlled Processes as a Dynamic Resource Competition
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational ecology to study the interaction between automatic and controlled cognitive processes. In ecology, the competition between fast-reproducing, opportunistic species (r-strategists) and slower, efficient species (K-strategists) is successfully modeled using coupled differential equations (e.g., Lotka-Volterra models) to predict outcomes like dominance or coexistence based on initial conditions and resource availability. By analogizing automatic processes to r-strategists and controlled processes to K-strategists competing for a shared pool of attentional/working memory resources, we can develop a formal computational model. This model will use differential equations to simulate how the early automatic activation and later controlled activation dynamically compete or combine over time, generating testable predictions for behavioral metrics such as error rates and reaction time distributions under varying resource constraints.

ideas.title: Multi-Ring Attention via Frequency-Domain Filter Banks
ideas.description: This research investigates scaling attention complexity by processing multiple concentric or disjoint rings in feature space simultaneously, inspired by filter banks in signal processing. The analogous solution from audio engineering uses parallel filters tuned to different frequency bands, which are processed and recombined. Adapted to this context, we propose constructing a bank of attention mechanisms, each attending to a specific radial band (ring) in the spatial or feature domain. These parallel attention 'filters' operate concurrently, and their outputs are integrated via a learned gating network. This allows the model to dynamically scale its representational capacity by emphasizing different rings or combinations thereof, enabling more efficient and structured multi-scale reasoning compared to monolithic attention over the entire field.

ideas.title: Dissect the controlled activation phase using high-resolution event segmentation from fluid dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from experimental fluid dynamics, where high-speed imaging and event segmentation are used to decompose complex, continuous flows into discrete functional phases. The analogous problem of analyzing multi-stage turbulent flow is solved using techniques like Particle Image Velocimetry and algorithms that identify sharp transitions in flow properties to define phases like vortex formation and dissipation. Adapted to cognitive neuroscience, this approach would use high-temporal-resolution neural measures (MEG/iEEG) during the later, controlled activation phase. The core method involves applying event segmentation algorithms to neural time-series data to identify sharp transitions in oscillatory power or connectivity, thereby parsing the seemingly sustained 'controlled phase' into its constituent sub-processes (e.g., maintenance, monitoring, adjustment) for detailed, stage-specific functional analysis.

ideas.title: Phase-Locked TMS Disruption Using Cardiac Electrophysiology Timing Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from cardiac electrophysiology, where interventions are precisely timed to specific phases of an intrinsic electrical cycle for selective disruption. In cardiac ablation, energy is delivered during precise temporal windows of the cardiac cycle to ablate pathological tissue while sparing normal function. Adapted to neuroscience, this involves using real-time neurophysiological feedback (e.g., EEG-derived motor-related cortical potentials or EMG onset) to trigger TMS pulses at a fixed, pre-determined latency. This phase-locked TMS would allow selective disruption of either the early preparatory/initiation phases or the late execution phases of motor activation, depending on the chosen trigger latency, thereby temporally dissecting the motor cascade with high precision.

ideas.title: Evolutionarily Constrained Generative Modeling of Neural Selection Timelines
ideas.description: This research uses generative neural models to simulate neurodevelopmental trajectories, but crucially subjects them to an explicit, interpretable evolutionary selection process defined by biophysical and informational constraints. The system separates the generative capacity of the network from the selection mechanism, allowing for causal experiments where specific selection pressures (e.g., for energy efficiency or noise robustness) are manipulated. This enables direct testing of hypotheses about which constraints drive the early emergence of stable core circuits versus the later development of adaptive modulatory systems, producing more biologically plausible and mechanistically insightful models of developmental timing.

ideas.title: Applying Evolutionary Game Theory Frameworks to Model Neural Selection Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from evolutionary game theory to the original computational modeling problem. In evolutionary biology, replicator equations model how strategies are selected in a population based on relative fitness. We adapt this by framing the learning dynamics of a neural network as an evolutionary game: different nascent representations (e.g., early vs. late modulation patterns) are competing strategies within the network's parameter space. Their 'fitness' is determined by their contribution to minimizing the loss function. By implementing a computational model based on replicator dynamics, we can simulate and analyze the conditions (e.g., learning rate, network architecture, noise) that lead to the emergence and stabilization of early exploratory features versus later, highly task-modulated features, providing a formal theory for the selection process in artificial neural systems.

papers.abstract: Attentional modulations of event‐related brain potentials (ERPs) were measured when subjects were cued to attend to a visual quadrant or to a ring‐shaped region of visual space to detect infrequently presented targets within the attended region.
